works.authorDate: Anonymous; traditionally Vyasa, redacted into the Mahabharata · c. 200 BC – 200 AD
works.form: Theological dialogue in 700 verses, 18 chapters · Vedānta / classical Hinduism
works.tagline: Three yogas — knowledge, action, devotion — converge on a personal Lord who is also the impersonal absolute

works.title: The Dhammapada
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to the Buddha, compiled by the early sangha) · c. 3rd century BC (Pali recension; verses likely older)
works.form: Verse anthology, 26 chapters · Theravāda Buddhism (received across all Buddhist traditions)
works.tagline: No self, no enduring substance, no creator — liberation is the cessation of craving in this very life

works.title: Tao Te Ching
works.authorDate: Attributed to Laozi (Lao Tzu); likely composite, possibly c. 4th–3rd century BC · c. 4th century BC (received text); Guodian bamboo slips c. 300 BC
works.form: Aphoristic poetry, 81 short chapters · Daoism / Daojia
works.tagline: The dao that can be named is not the eternal dao — the way of nature is yielding, paradoxical, and beyond determinate concepts

works.title: Zhuangzi — Inner Chapters
works.authorDate: Zhuang Zhou (with later editorial layers; Inner Chapters most likely by his hand) · c. late 4th century BC
works.form: Philosophical narrative, parable, and dialogue · Daoism / Daojia
works.tagline: Perspective-relativity, the dream of the butterfly, and the great clod — Daoism's most playful and most radical book

works.title: The Republic
works.form: Philosophical dialogue in ten books · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: Reality is the Forms; the visible world is their shadow; the just city is the just soul writ large

works.title: Timaeus (Late)
works.form: Cosmological monologue framed as a dialogue · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: A "likely story" of how a rational Demiurge ordered the receptacle of matter into a living cosmos modelled on the Forms

works.title: Nicomachean Ethics
works.authorDate: Aristotle (edited by Nicomachus) · c. 340 BC (lecture notes, Lyceum period)
works.form: Treatise in ten books · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelianism
works.tagline: Eudaimonia is the activity of soul in accordance with virtue, in a complete life — the foundation of every later virtue ethics

works.title: Letter to Menoeceus
works.form: Personal letter (one of three surviving complete letters) · Hellenistic philosophy / Epicureanism
works.tagline: Death is nothing to us; the gods do not concern themselves with us; pleasure rightly understood is the absence of pain

works.title: Meditations
works.form: Personal notebooks in twelve books · Roman Stoicism
works.tagline: The cosmos is a single rational order, the self is governed by reason, and what is not in one's power is not one's concern

works.title: Confessions (Early)
works.form: Spiritual autobiography in dialogue with God · Latin Christianity / Augustinianism
works.tagline: A restless heart finds rest in God — and along the way invents Western philosophical autobiography, the modern self, and the philosophy of time

works.title: Summa Theologiae
works.authorDate: Thomas Aquinas · 1265–1274 (left incomplete at Aquinas's death)
works.form: Disputational treatise — questions, articles, objections, replies · Western Catholicism / Scholasticism / Thomism
works.tagline: Faith and reason in concord — Aristotle baptised, Augustine systematised, the act of being made foundational

works.title: On the Bondage of the Will
works.form: Theological polemic — point-by-point reply · Reformation theology / Lutheranism
works.tagline: The human will is bound under sin; salvation is by grace alone, without contribution from the creature

works.title: Institutes of the Christian Religion (Late)
works.form: Systematic theological treatise in four books, 80 chapters · Reformed Protestantism / Calvinism
works.tagline: Knowledge of God and of ourselves are inseparable — and both are the gift of the Word, by the Spirit, to fallen creatures

works.title: Meditations on First Philosophy
works.authorDate: René Descartes · 1641 (Latin); French translation by Duc de Luynes 1647
works.form: Six first-person meditations with appended Objections and Replies · Early modern rationalism / continental philosophy
works.tagline: Cogito ergo sum — radical doubt as method, the thinking thing as foundation, the world re-erected on the certainty of consciousness

works.title: Pensées
works.authorDate: Blaise Pascal · c. 1657–62 (Pascal d. 1662); first published 1670
works.form: Fragments — sometimes single sentences, sometimes substantial essays · Catholic Jansenism / Christian apologetics
works.tagline: Man is a thinking reed in an infinite cosmos — wagering on God is rational, the heart has reasons reason does not know

works.title: Ethics
works.authorDate: Baruch Spinoza · completed c. 1675; published posthumously 1677
works.form: Geometrical treatise — definitions, axioms, propositions, demonstrations, scholia · Early modern rationalism / Spinozism
works.tagline: Deus sive natura — God or nature, one infinite substance, expressed in infinite attributes, of which we know two — extension and thought

works.title: An Enquiry Concerning Human Understanding (Late)
works.authorDate: David Hume · 1748 (first published as Philosophical Essays Concerning Human Understanding)
works.form: Philosophical essays in twelve sections · British empiricism / Scottish Enlightenment
works.tagline: All knowledge starts from impressions; causation is custom, not necessary connection; reason is and ought to be the slave of the passions

works.title: Critique of Pure Reason
works.authorDate: Immanuel Kant · 1781 (A edition); 1787 (B edition, substantially revised)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise · Modern German philosophy / transcendental idealism
works.tagline: The "Copernican revolution" — the conditions of any possible experience are supplied by the mind, not by things in themselves

works.title: Phenomenology of Spirit (Early)
works.authorDate: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel · 1806–07 (finished as Napoleon entered Jena)
works.form: Systematic philosophical narrative in eight stages · German idealism / Hegelianism
works.tagline: Consciousness ascends through self-correcting stages of error toward Spirit's self-recognition in history

works.title: Fear and Trembling (Early)
works.authorDate: Søren Kierkegaard (under the pseudonym Johannes de silentio) · 1843
works.form: Pseudonymous philosophical-religious meditation in four problems · Christian existentialism / Danish Lutheranism
works.tagline: Faith requires a "teleological suspension of the ethical" — Abraham is great because he believes the absurd

works.title: On the Origin of Species
works.authorDate: Charles Darwin · 1859 (first edition); five subsequent revised editions in Darwin's lifetime
works.form: Scientific treatise with extensive empirical case studies · Modern biology / scientific naturalism
works.tagline: Descent with modification by natural selection — the most consequential single book in the natural sciences

works.title: Thus Spoke Zarathustra
works.authorDate: Friedrich Nietzsche · 1883 (parts I, II); 1884 (III); 1885 (IV, private printing)
works.form: Philosophical fiction in mock-biblical prose, with songs and parables · Continental philosophy / Nietzsche's post-Christian project
works.tagline: God is dead — the Übermensch is the meaning of the earth — life affirms its own eternal recurrence

works.title: Tractatus Logico-Philosophicus (Early)
works.authorDate: Ludwig Wittgenstein · 1918 (drafted in the trenches); 1921 (German pub.); 1922 (Ogden English ed.)
works.form: Numbered propositions (1, 1.1, 1.11, 1.12...) with seven theses · Analytic philosophy / logical atomism
works.tagline: The world is the totality of facts, not of things — and whereof one cannot speak, thereof one must be silent

works.title: Being and Time (Early)
works.authorDate: Martin Heidegger · 1927 (Jahrbuch für Philosophie publication; only Divisions I and II of the planned three completed)
works.form: Systematic phenomenological treatise · Continental philosophy / phenomenology / fundamental ontology
works.tagline: Being is the question; Dasein is the being whose being is in question for it; time is the horizon of meaning

works.title: The Myth of Sisyphus
works.form: Philosophical essay in four chapters and an appendix on Kafka · French existentialism / absurdism
works.tagline: There is only one really serious philosophical problem — suicide. The answer is to imagine Sisyphus happy.

works.title: Being and Nothingness
works.form: Systematic phenomenological treatise in four parts · French existentialism / phenomenological ontology
works.tagline: Being-for-itself is the freedom that always precedes essence — we are condemned to be free, and bad faith is the flight from this freedom

works.title: Mere Christianity
works.authorDate: C. S. Lewis · 1941–44 (BBC talks); 1952 (single-volume book form)
works.form: Popular apologetic essays in four books · Anglican Christianity / classical Christian apologetics
works.tagline: The "trilemma" of Lord, liar, or lunatic; the moral law as evidence of a moral Lawgiver; the common Christianity beneath denominational difference

works.title: Philosophical Investigations (Late)
works.authorDate: Ludwig Wittgenstein · c. 1929–49 (drafted across two decades); 1953 (posthumous publication, ed. Anscombe & Rhees)
works.form: Numbered remarks (§1–§693) plus Part II / "Philosophy of Psychology — A Fragment" · Analytic philosophy / ordinary-language philosophy
works.tagline: Meaning is use — language is a family of games — philosophy leaves everything as it is, and the picture of the inner that held us captive is dismantled

works.title: The Hebrew Bible (Tanakh)
works.authorDate: Anonymous / composite (many authors, redactors, scribal communities over a millennium) · c. 1200 BC (oldest core) – c. 165 BC (Daniel); canon stabilised c. 100 AD
works.form: Composite scripture in 24 books — Torah (5), Nevi'im (8), Ketuvim (11) · Judaism (read also as the Old Testament in all branches of Christianity)
works.tagline: One God, creator of heaven and earth; a chosen people; a covenant with concrete moral demands; history as the arena of divine action

works.title: The New Testament
works.authorDate: Anonymous and pseudonymous; the named Pauline letters (Romans, 1–2 Cor, Gal, Phil, Phlm, 1 Thess) are widely accepted as authentically Paul's · c. 50–110 AD; canon stabilised by late 4th century
works.form: Gospels, historical narrative, occasional letters, apocalyptic · Christianity (all branches)
works.tagline: The Word became flesh — the cross and resurrection of Jesus reorder time, matter, observer, and information at once

works.title: The Quran
works.authorDate: Considered by Muslims the direct word of God; transmitted through Muhammad; collected under 'Uthmān (c. 650) · c. 610–632 AD (the period of the Prophet's mission); 'Uthmānic codex c. 650
works.form: 114 sūras (chapters), c. 6,236 āyāt (verses) · Islam (all branches)
works.tagline: There is no god but God; the recitation is the direct, untranslatable speech of God; submission (islām) is the human posture before the divine

works.title: The Analects
works.authorDate: Compiled by Confucius's disciples and their disciples · Compiled c. 5th–3rd century BC; core sayings reflect Confucius (551–479 BC)
works.form: Twenty books of brief sayings, dialogues, and anecdotes · Confucianism / Ru tradition
works.tagline: Ren, li, junzi — the cultivation of humane character through ritual propriety makes social order possible

works.title: Metaphysics
works.authorDate: Aristotle (compiled posthumously by Andronicus of Rhodes c. 70 BC) · c. 350 BC (lecture notes, second Athenian period)
works.form: Treatise in fourteen books (Α–Ν), assembled from lecture courses · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelianism
works.tagline: Being is said in many ways — substance is the focal sense — and the unmoved mover is the eternal actuality at the apex of nature

works.title: Leviathan
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise in four parts · Early modern political philosophy / materialist naturalism
works.tagline: The state of nature is solitary, poor, nasty, brutish, and short; the social contract erects the Leviathan to keep us out of it

works.title: An Essay Concerning Human Understanding
works.authorDate: John Locke · 1689 (first ed.); fourth ed. with significant revisions 1700
works.form: Philosophical treatise in four books · British empiricism / Enlightenment epistemology
works.tagline: The mind at birth is a tabula rasa; all ideas come from sensation and reflection; certainty is bounded, but knowledge is real

works.title: Philosophiæ Naturalis Principia Mathematica
works.authorDate: Isaac Newton · 1687 (first ed.); 1713, 1726 (second and third revised eds)
works.form: Mathematical-physical treatise in three books, with Definitions, Laws of Motion, and General Scholium · Early modern natural philosophy / classical physics
works.tagline: Absolute space and time; universal gravitation; the system of the world deduced from three laws — the founding text of mathematical physics

works.title: Capital, Volume I (Late)
works.authorDate: Karl Marx · 1867 (German first ed.); Volume II 1885, Volume III 1894 (posthumous, ed. Engels)
works.form: Critique of political economy in seven parts · Dialectical materialism / Marxism / classical socialist political economy
works.tagline: The commodity form is the cell of capitalist society — value, labour, surplus, accumulation, and crisis follow from its dialectical analysis

works.title: On Liberty
works.form: Philosophical essay in five chapters · British liberalism / utilitarianism
works.tagline: The sole end for which mankind are warranted in interfering with the liberty of action of any of their number is self-protection

works.title: The Second Sex
works.form: Philosophical-anthropological treatise in two volumes · French existentialism / philosophical feminism
works.tagline: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman — the founding text of philosophical feminism, applying existentialist freedom to the situation of women

works.title: Phaedo
works.form: Philosophical dialogue framed as Phaedo's narration of Socrates's last day · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: Philosophy is preparation for death — and four arguments that the soul, being akin to the Forms, must be immortal

works.title: City of God (Late)
works.authorDate: Augustine of Hippo · 413–426 AD (composed in stages over thirteen years)
works.form: Theological treatise in twenty-two books · Latin Christianity / political theology
works.tagline: Two cities, the earthly and the heavenly, intermingled through history — the city of God is the people whose love is God's

works.title: Utilitarianism
works.form: Philosophical essay in five chapters · British utilitarianism / classical liberalism
works.tagline: Actions are right in proportion as they tend to promote happiness — and pleasures differ in kind, not only in quantity

works.title: The Communist Manifesto (Early)
works.authorDate: Karl Marx and Friedrich Engels · February 1848 (commissioned by the Communist League, London)
works.form: Political pamphlet in four sections · Marxism / revolutionary socialism
works.tagline: The history of all hitherto existing society is the history of class struggles — workers of the world, unite

works.title: The Consolation of Philosophy
works.authorDate: Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius · c. 524 AD (in prison at Pavia, awaiting execution by Theodoric)
works.form: Prosimetric dialogue in five books (alternating prose and verse) · Late antique Christian Platonism
works.tagline: Lady Philosophy consoles a doomed man — fortune is fickle, providence is just, eternity is the simultaneous and complete possession of unending life

works.title: Proslogion
works.form: Twenty-six chapters of meditative prayer-and-argument · Medieval Christian theology / scholasticism
works.tagline: God is "that than which nothing greater can be thought" — and from this single thought, the ontological argument for his existence follows

works.title: A Theory of Justice
works.form: Systematic political-philosophical treatise in three parts · Anglo-American liberal political philosophy
works.tagline: Justice is fairness — the principles rational persons would choose behind a veil of ignorance, ignorant of their own social position

works.title: The Guide of the Perplexed
works.form: Philosophical letter-treatise in three parts · Medieval Jewish philosophy / Aristotelian falsafa
works.tagline: Reason and revelation in concord — God is purely incorporeal, the Torah's anthropomorphisms are figurative, and the perplexed reader can be guided

works.title: Cartesian Meditations (Late)
works.form: Five meditations modelled on Descartes's six · Transcendental phenomenology
works.tagline: Husserl rereads Descartes as the inaugurator of phenomenology — the transcendental ego, intersubjectivity, and the constitution of the world

works.title: The Enneads
works.authorDate: Plotinus (edited by Porphyry c. 301) · Composed c. 254–270 AD; edited by Porphyry c. 301
works.form: Fifty-four philosophical treatises arranged in six "Enneads" (groups of nine) · Late antique philosophy / Neo-Platonism
works.tagline: The One emanates Intellect, which emanates Soul, which emanates Nature — and the philosophical life is the soul's ascent back to its source

works.title: Yoga Sutras
works.authorDate: Patañjali (the historical author or compiler; possibly composite) · c. 2nd century BC – 4th century AD (composite redaction likely)
works.form: 196 aphoristic sutras in four pādas (chapters) · Hindu philosophy / one of the six classical darshanas
works.tagline: Yoga is the cessation of the modifications of the mind (yogaś citta-vṛtti-nirodhaḥ) — the eightfold path leads to liberating samādhi

works.title: An Inquiry into the Nature and Causes of the Wealth of Nations (Late)
works.authorDate: Adam Smith · 1776 (first ed.); five revised editions in Smith's lifetime
works.form: Treatise on political economy in five books · Scottish Enlightenment / classical political economy
works.tagline: The division of labour, the invisible hand, and the wealth of nations grounded in productive labour rather than gold

works.title: Process and Reality (Late)
works.authorDate: Alfred North Whitehead · 1929 (delivered as Gifford Lectures, Edinburgh, 1927–28)
works.form: Systematic cosmological treatise in five parts · Process philosophy / philosophy of organism
works.tagline: Reality consists of actual occasions of experience — drops of process — concrescent with their relations; God is the principle of concretion

works.title: The World as Will and Representation
works.authorDate: Arthur Schopenhauer · 1818 (first ed.); 1844 (expanded with second volume); 1859 (final third edition)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in four books, with supplementary second volume · German post-Kantian philosophy / pessimist metaphysics
works.tagline: The world we perceive is representation; the inner thing-in-itself is blind, ceaseless Will; release comes through aesthetic contemplation and ascetic denial

works.title: Letters and Papers from Prison (Late)
works.authorDate: Dietrich Bonhoeffer · 1943–45 (Tegel and Flossenbürg prisons); 1951 (first German edition by Eberhard Bethge)
works.form: Correspondence, theological essays, poetry, occasional fragments · Confessing Church / twentieth-century Christian dissent
works.tagline: Religionless Christianity, costly grace, and the etsi deus non daretur — to live before God as if there were no God

works.title: The Kingdom of God Is Within You (Late)
works.authorDate: Leo Tolstoy · Written 1890–93; banned in Russia, published 1894 in Berlin
works.form: Religious-political treatise in twelve chapters · Late Tolstoyan Christian anarchism / Christian non-violence
works.tagline: The Sermon on the Mount taken at its word — non-resistance to evil dissolves both state coercion and ecclesiastical hierarchy

works.title: Mūlamadhyamakakārikā
works.form: Twenty-seven chapters of philosophical verse, c. 450 stanzas · Mahāyāna Buddhism / Madhyamaka
works.tagline: Everything that arises does so dependently — nothing has intrinsic nature (svabhāva), emptiness (śūnyatā) is the truth of dependent origination

works.title: Letter on Humanism (Late)
works.authorDate: Martin Heidegger · 1946 (drafted as a letter to Jean Beaufret); 1947 (published)
works.form: Philosophical letter / treatise · Continental philosophy / late Heidegger
works.tagline: After the Kehre — language is the house of being, the human is the shepherd of being, and humanism is the metaphysical forgetfulness to be overcome

works.title: The Varieties of Religious Experience
works.form: Twenty Gifford Lectures with extensive case studies · American pragmatism / psychology of religion
works.tagline: Religion is real because its effects are real — judged not by origins but by fruits, the "more" disclosed in religious experience deserves respect

works.title: Metaphysics of The Book of Healing (Late)
works.authorDate: Ibn Sīnā (Avicenna) · c. 1014–1027 (compiled during Avicenna's years at Hamadan and Isfahan)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in ten books · Medieval Islamic philosophy / Peripatetic falsafa
works.tagline: The distinction of essence and existence, the necessary existent, the giver of forms — Avicenna's metaphysics shaped both Aquinas and the entire later Islamic tradition

works.title: An Essay in Aid of a Grammar of Assent (Late)
works.form: Philosophical-theological treatise in two parts · Nineteenth-century Catholic intellectual revival
works.tagline: The illative sense — how reasonable persons reach certitude on questions where formal demonstration is impossible, especially religious belief

works.title: Symposium
works.form: Philosophical dialogue framed as nested narration · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: Eros is the ascent — from beautiful bodies, to beautiful souls, to the Beautiful itself — Diotima's ladder of love

works.title: De Anima
works.form: Treatise in three books · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelianism
works.tagline: The soul is the form of the living body — and the active intellect is the puzzle that defined medieval philosophy

works.title: A Treatise Concerning the Principles of Human Knowledge (Early)
works.form: Philosophical treatise in 156 numbered sections · British empiricism / philosophical idealism
works.tagline: Esse est percipi — to be is to be perceived; there is no inert mindless matter; only minds and ideas exist

works.title: Monadology (Late)
works.authorDate: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz · 1714 (written in French for Prince Eugene of Savoy); published 1720 in German
works.form: 90 numbered philosophical paragraphs · Early modern rationalism / pluralist idealism
works.tagline: The world is composed of simple substances — monads — windowless and active, each mirroring the whole; pre-established harmony coordinates them all

works.title: Fides et Ratio (Late)
works.authorDate: Pope John Paul II (Karol Wojtyła) · 14 September 1998 (encyclical letter)
works.form: Papal encyclical letter in seven chapters · Roman Catholic magisterial theology / Lublin Thomism
works.tagline: Faith and reason are the two wings on which the human spirit rises to the contemplation of truth — neither can fly alone

works.title: Apology (Early)
works.authorDate: Plato · c. 399–395 BC (shortly after Socrates's death)
works.form: Forensic speech, three parts (defence, counter-penalty, last words) · Classical Greek philosophy / Socratic tradition
works.tagline: The unexamined life is not worth living — and the philosopher chooses death over the surrender of philosophy

works.title: Phaedrus (Late)
works.form: Two-character philosophical dialogue · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: The soul is a charioteer drawn by two horses — and writing is a poor cousin to living speech

works.title: Politics
works.form: Treatise in eight books · Classical Greek political philosophy
works.tagline: Man is by nature a political animal — the polis exists for the sake of the good life, not merely life

works.title: Outlines of Pyrrhonism
works.form: Philosophical-doxographical treatise in three books · Late antique philosophy / Pyrrhonist scepticism
works.tagline: For every argument there is an equal counter-argument — and from equipollence comes suspension and tranquillity

works.title: On the Nature of the Gods (Late)
works.form: Philosophical dialogue in three books · Roman philosophy / Academic scepticism
works.tagline: A philosophical conversation between Epicurean, Stoic, and Academic representatives — the founding modern reference for natural theology

works.title: Brahma Sutra Bhāṣya
works.form: Sūtra commentary (bhāṣya) — line-by-line exegesis with extended discussions · Hindu philosophy / Advaita Vedānta
works.tagline: Brahman alone is real; the world is appearance; the Self is Brahman — and liberation comes through knowledge alone

works.title: Abhidharmakośa
works.form: Verse text in eight chapters with prose auto-commentary (bhāṣya) · Sarvāstivāda Buddhism / Abhidharma
works.tagline: The systematic catalogue of dharmas — moments of experience as the irreducible elements of reality

works.title: Visuddhimagga
works.authorDate: Buddhaghosa · c. 430 AD (composed at the Mahāvihāra monastery, Anurādhapura, Sri Lanka)
works.form: Comprehensive treatise in twenty-three chapters · Theravāda Buddhism
works.tagline: Virtue, meditation, wisdom — the threefold path of purification leading to liberation, systematically expounded

works.title: The Heart Sutra
works.authorDate: Anonymous (Mahāyāna tradition; some scholars argue for a Chinese composition c. 7th century) · c. 600 AD (extant form); verses possibly earlier
works.form: Short sūtra — about 260 Chinese characters in the standard recension · Mahāyāna Buddhism / Prajñāpāramitā literature
works.tagline: Form is emptiness; emptiness is form — the compressed essence of the Perfection of Wisdom tradition

works.title: The Platform Sutra of the Sixth Patriarch
works.authorDate: Hui-neng (638–713), as transmitted by Fa-hai · c. 780 AD (Dunhuang manuscript); refined recensions through 13th century
works.form: Recorded sermons and dialogues in ten chapters · Chan / Zen Buddhism
works.tagline: Sudden enlightenment — original nature is already buddha — and Hui-neng's "no-mind" instructions to the Southern School

works.title: Mencius
works.authorDate: Meng Ke (Mencius); compiled by his disciples · c. late 4th century BC (compiled shortly after his death c. 289 BC)
works.form: Seven books of dialogues and aphorisms · Confucianism / Ru tradition
works.tagline: Human nature is fundamentally good — the four sprouts of compassion, shame, deference, and judgement need only cultivation

works.title: Xunzi
works.form: Thirty-two philosophical chapters · Confucianism / "realist" Confucian tradition
works.tagline: Human nature is bad — ritual, learning, and authority make us good — and Heaven follows constant patterns indifferent to human prayers

works.title: I Ching
works.authorDate: Anonymous / composite (traditional attribution to King Wen and Confucius; the Ten Wings to the Confucian school) · c. 9th–8th c. BC (core hexagrams); c. 4th c. BC (Ten Wings); standard form c. 200 BC
works.form: 64 hexagrams with judgements and line statements + Ten Wings commentaries · Chinese cosmology / classical Confucian and Daoist canon
works.tagline: The patterns of change in the cosmos read through sixty-four hexagrams — the oldest extant systematic Chinese cosmology

works.title: On the Trinity (Late)
works.authorDate: Augustine of Hippo · c. 399–419 (composed across two decades)
works.form: Theological treatise in fifteen books · Latin Christianity / Augustinian theology
works.tagline: The Trinity in itself, the Trinity in the human mind — the most sustained patristic analysis of God as three persons, one being

works.title: Summa Contra Gentiles (Early)
works.form: Philosophical-theological treatise in four books · Western Catholicism / Scholasticism / Thomism
works.tagline: A philosophical case for Christian truth addressed to non-Christian readers — three books from reason, the fourth from revelation

works.title: The German Sermons (Late)
works.authorDate: Meister Eckhart (Eckhart von Hochheim) · c. 1295–1327 (preached in Strasbourg, Cologne, and elsewhere)
works.form: Vernacular sermons — over a hundred survive in various manuscript collections · Christian mysticism / German Dominican spirituality
works.tagline: The ground of the soul is the ground of God — and the divine birth happens in the still point of the soul

works.title: The Mystical Theology
works.authorDate: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite (probably a Syrian Christian theologian, c. 500 AD) · c. 500 AD (probably Syria)
works.form: Short theological treatise in five chapters · Eastern Christianity / Christian apophatic mysticism
works.tagline: God is beyond every name and every concept — the soul ascends by unknowing into the divine darkness

works.title: Revelations of Divine Love
works.authorDate: Julian of Norwich · May 1373 (the showings); short text c. 1380; long text c. 1395 (revised over twenty years)
works.form: Visionary theological text in 86 chapters (long version) · English mystical tradition / fourteenth-century vernacular theology
works.tagline: All shall be well — Jesus as mother, sin as nothing of being, and the universe held in the divine love that is the hazelnut

works.title: The Imitation of Christ
works.authorDate: Thomas à Kempis (traditional attribution; sometimes attributed to Geert Groote or composite) · c. 1418–1427 (Mount St Agnes monastery, Zwolle, Netherlands)
works.form: Devotional treatise in four books · Devotio Moderna / Late medieval Christian spirituality
works.tagline: Christ's way is the cross — and the imitation of Christ is the way of the soul to God, in concrete daily practice

works.title: Faṣl al-Maqāl (The Decisive Treatise) (Late)
works.form: Legal-philosophical treatise · Medieval Islamic philosophy / Andalusian Peripatetic falsafa
works.tagline: Philosophy and scripture are two paths to the same truth — when they appear to conflict, the scriptural text requires allegorical interpretation

works.title: The Incoherence of the Philosophers
works.authorDate: Abū Ḥāmid Muḥammad al-Ghazālī · 1095 (Baghdad, immediately before his crisis and withdrawal)
works.form: Philosophical-theological treatise in twenty discussions · Medieval Islamic theology / Ash'arite kalam
works.tagline: The philosophers contradict themselves on twenty crucial points — and on three of them (the eternity of the world, divine knowledge of particulars, bodily resurrection) they contradict the Quran

works.title: Mathnawi (Late)
works.authorDate: Jalāl al-Dīn Muḥammad Rūmī · c. 1258–1273 (Konya, dictated in Persian over fifteen years)
works.form: Mathnawī (rhyming-couplet) verse epic in six volumes, c. 26,000 couplets · Persian Sufism / philosophical mysticism
works.tagline: The reed cut from the reed-bed cries out — and the longing for return is itself the way back

works.title: The Zohar
works.authorDate: Traditionally Shimon bar Yochai (2nd c. AD); modern scholarship attributes to Moses de León c. 1280 · c. 1280 (Castile, Spain); first published in print 1558
works.form: Commentary on the Torah in mystical-narrative form · Jewish mysticism / Kabbalah
works.tagline: The Torah read through the ten sefirot — God's ten emanations through which creation, history, and the soul's ascent are structured

works.title: De Cive (Early)
works.authorDate: Thomas Hobbes · 1642 (Latin, Paris); English translation by Hobbes himself 1651
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise in three parts (Liberty, Dominion, Religion) · Early modern political philosophy / materialist naturalism
works.tagline: The state of nature is war; the social contract creates sovereign authority; the duty of religion is consistent with civil peace

works.title: Two Treatises of Government (Late)
works.authorDate: John Locke · Written c. 1679–82; published anonymously 1689
works.form: Two-part political treatise (First Treatise: refutation of Filmer; Second Treatise: positive doctrine) · Early modern liberal political philosophy
works.tagline: Government rests on the consent of the governed — and when it fails to protect life, liberty, and property, the people may resist

works.title: A Treatise of Human Nature (Early)
works.authorDate: David Hume · Books I & II 1739; Book III 1740 (anonymously; Hume aged 28)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in three books · British empiricism / Scottish Enlightenment
works.tagline: Reason is and ought to be the slave of the passions — and the most rigorous Newtonian science of human nature

works.title: Dialogues Concerning Natural Religion (Late)
works.authorDate: David Hume · Drafted 1751–61; revised continuously; published posthumously 1779
works.form: Philosophical dialogue in twelve parts · British empiricism / Enlightenment philosophy of religion
works.tagline: The design argument refuted, dogmatic theism shaken, but the question of God's existence left genuinely open

works.title: Critique of Practical Reason (Late)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise · Modern German philosophy / Kantian transcendental idealism
works.tagline: Two things fill the mind with ever new and increasing admiration — the starry heavens above me, and the moral law within me

works.title: Groundwork of the Metaphysics of Morals
works.form: Philosophical treatise in three sections · Modern German philosophy / Kantian ethics
works.tagline: Act only according to that maxim by which you can at the same time will that it should become a universal law

works.title: Critique of Judgment (Late)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in two parts · Modern German philosophy / Kantian aesthetics and philosophy of biology
works.tagline: Aesthetic judgement and teleological judgement bridge the gap between theoretical reason and practical reason

works.title: The Sickness Unto Death (Late)
works.form: Two-part philosophical-theological treatise · Christian existentialism / Danish Lutheranism
works.tagline: Despair is the sickness unto death — the self in misrelation to itself before God

works.title: Either/Or (Early)
works.authorDate: Søren Kierkegaard (under the editorship of Victor Eremita) · 1843
works.form: Two-volume literary-philosophical work · Christian existentialism / Danish Lutheranism
works.tagline: The aesthetic life of immediate pleasure vs the ethical life of commitment — and the choice that constitutes the self

works.title: System of Transcendental Idealism (Early)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise · German Idealism
works.tagline: The history of self-consciousness — from nature's production of mind to mind's recognition of itself in art

works.title: A Vindication of the Rights of Woman
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise in thirteen chapters · Enlightenment radicalism / proto-feminist political philosophy
works.tagline: Women are not naturally inferior — only made so by inadequate education; reason is the same in both sexes

works.title: Democracy in America
works.authorDate: Alexis de Tocqueville · Volume I 1835; Volume II 1840 (based on Tocqueville's 1831–32 American journey)
works.form: Sociological-political treatise in two volumes · French liberal political thought / sociology before sociology
works.tagline: Equality of conditions is the providential fact of the modern age — and democracy's greatest danger is the tyranny of the majority

works.title: Theses on Feuerbach (Early)
works.authorDate: Karl Marx · 1845 (notebook fragments, published posthumously by Engels in 1888 with slight editorial changes)
works.form: Eleven aphoristic theses · Dialectical materialism / Marxism
works.tagline: Philosophers have only interpreted the world in various ways; the point, however, is to change it

works.title: The Problems of Philosophy (Early)
works.form: Introductory philosophical treatise in fifteen chapters · Analytic philosophy / British empiricism
works.tagline: A short introduction to philosophical questions about knowledge, perception, induction, and the value of philosophy itself

works.title: The Foundations of Arithmetic
works.form: Logico-philosophical treatise · Analytic philosophy / logicism / philosophy of mathematics
works.tagline: Arithmetic is reducible to logic — numbers are not subjective ideas but objective abstract entities accessible to reason

works.title: Experience and Nature (Late)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in ten chapters · American pragmatism / naturalism
works.tagline: Experience and nature are not opposed — experience is how we know nature, and nature is what experience is of

works.title: How to Make Our Ideas Clear
works.authorDate: Charles Sanders Peirce · 1878 (Popular Science Monthly, January)
works.form: Philosophical essay · American pragmatism (founding text)
works.tagline: Consider what effects might conceivably have practical bearings — your conception of those effects is the whole of your conception of the object

works.title: What Is Metaphysics? (Early)
works.form: Philosophical lecture · Continental philosophy / phenomenology
works.tagline: Why is there something rather than nothing? — the question of being approached through the experience of dread (Angst)

works.title: Phenomenology of Perception
works.form: Systematic phenomenological treatise · French phenomenology / embodied cognition
works.tagline: The body is the medium of perception — neither pure consciousness nor pure object, but the lived body engaged with the world

works.title: The Order of Things
works.form: Archaeological-philosophical treatise · French post-structuralism / archaeology of knowledge
works.tagline: Man is an invention of recent date — and one perhaps nearing its end

works.title: Of Grammatology
works.form: Two-part philosophical treatise (theoretical + reading of Rousseau) · French post-structuralism / deconstruction
works.tagline: There is no outside-text — and the metaphysics of presence that privileges speech over writing is undone by writing's own structure

works.title: Totality and Infinity (Early)
works.form: Phenomenological-ethical treatise in four sections · Twentieth-century French Jewish philosophy / ethical phenomenology
works.tagline: The face of the Other places an infinite ethical demand on the self — ethics is first philosophy

works.title: The Human Condition
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise · Twentieth-century political philosophy / Arendt's post-totalitarian thought
works.tagline: Labour, work, action — three distinct activities of the vita activa, and the modern reduction of action to making

works.title: On Certainty (Latest)
works.authorDate: Ludwig Wittgenstein · Written 1949–51 (in Wittgenstein's final eighteen months); published posthumously 1969
works.form: Aphoristic notebook entries — 676 numbered remarks · Analytic philosophy / late-late Wittgenstein
works.tagline: The foundations of empirical knowledge lie in our forms of life — and our framework certainties cannot themselves be doubted within the system that depends on them

works.title: Relativity: The Special and General Theory
works.form: Popular scientific exposition · Modern physics / philosophical-scientific worldview
works.tagline: Space and time are observer-relative; gravity is curvature of spacetime — Newtonian absolute space dissolved

works.title: Physics and Philosophy
works.form: Popular philosophical lectures · Modern physics / Copenhagen interpretation
works.tagline: The atom is not a thing — and the observer's knowledge is part of the physical situation; the Copenhagen interpretation defended

works.title: The Selfish Gene
works.form: Popular scientific exposition · Modern evolutionary biology / philosophical naturalism
works.tagline: Genes are the units of selection — and bodies are vehicles by which genes propagate themselves

works.title: Dialogue Concerning the Two Chief World Systems
works.authorDate: Galileo Galilei · 1632 (Florence; placed on the Index of Prohibited Books later that year)
works.form: Four-day philosophical dialogue between three speakers (Salviati, Sagredo, Simplicio) · Early modern natural philosophy / scientific revolution
works.tagline: A vernacular defence of heliocentrism — the book that brought Galileo before the Inquisition

works.title: Novum Organum
works.authorDate: Francis Bacon · 1620 (London; intended as Part II of the never-completed Instauratio Magna)
works.form: Aphorisms in two books · Early modern empiricism / scientific method
works.tagline: Knowledge is power — and the road to it is patient inductive observation, freed from the four Idols of the mind

works.title: The Structure of Scientific Revolutions
works.authorDate: Thomas S. Kuhn · 1962 (1st ed.); 1970 (2nd ed. with postscript); 1996 (3rd ed.)
works.form: Historical-philosophical treatise · Twentieth-century philosophy and history of science
works.tagline: Normal science under paradigms is punctuated by revolutionary changes that are not strictly cumulative — and competing paradigms are incommensurable

works.title: The Open Society and Its Enemies
works.authorDate: Karl R. Popper · Composed 1938–1943 in New Zealand exile; published 1945 (2 vols)
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise in two volumes · Twentieth-century political liberalism / philosophy of science applied to politics
works.tagline: The closed society of tribal traditions vs the open society of critical inquiry — and the historicist enemies that produce totalitarianism

works.title: On the Genealogy of Morality (Late)
works.form: Three connected essays · Continental philosophy / Nietzsche's mature critique of morality
works.tagline: Good/evil as slave-revolt in morality; bad conscience as turned-in cruelty; the ascetic ideal as the human will's defence against its own meaninglessness

works.title: Concluding Unscientific Postscript
works.authorDate: Søren Kierkegaard (under the pseudonym Johannes Climacus) · 1846
works.form: Pseudonymous philosophical treatise · Christian existentialism / Danish Lutheranism
works.tagline: Truth is subjectivity — and an objective approach to Christianity is precisely how one misses it

works.title: On Free Choice of the Will (Early)
works.authorDate: Augustine of Hippo · c. 387–395 (Book I in Rome 388; Books II–III at Hippo c. 391–395)
works.form: Three-book philosophical dialogue with his friend Evodius · Latin Christianity / Augustinian theology
works.tagline: God is not the author of evil — free will is; and yet the will's very freedom is itself a divine gift

works.title: Meno (Early)
works.form: Philosophical dialogue · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: Can virtue be taught? — and the slave-boy who, asked questions, recovers geometric truth he never learned

works.title: A History of Western Philosophy (Late)
works.form: Historical-philosophical survey · Twentieth-century analytic philosophy / popular philosophical history
works.tagline: Two millennia of Western philosophy narrated by a Nobel-laureate philosopher with strong opinions and clear English

works.title: The Courage to Be
works.form: Six-chapter philosophical-theological treatise · Twentieth-century philosophical theology / existentialist theology
works.tagline: Anxiety of fate, guilt, and meaninglessness is overcome by the courage to be — grounded in the God who appears when the God of theism has disappeared

works.title: After Virtue
works.authorDate: Alasdair MacIntyre · 1981 (1st ed.); 1984 (2nd ed.); 2007 (3rd ed., with new prologue)
works.form: Philosophical treatise in nineteen chapters · Twentieth-century moral philosophy / virtue ethics revival
works.tagline: Modern moral disagreement is interminable because we have inherited fragments of a tradition we no longer understand — recovery requires returning to Aristotle

works.title: Theory of Communicative Action
works.form: Two-volume systematic social-philosophical treatise · Frankfurt School / discourse theory / critical theory
works.tagline: Rationality is built into the structure of communication itself — and the lifeworld of democratic discourse is being colonised by instrumental system imperatives

works.title: Discipline and Punish (Late)
works.form: Genealogical study in four parts (Torture / Punishment / Discipline / Prison) · French post-structuralism / genealogical method
works.tagline: The body of the condemned moves from spectacular torture to disciplinary surveillance — and the panopticon's gaze becomes the modern soul

works.title: The Abolition of Man
works.authorDate: C. S. Lewis · 1943 (Riddell Memorial Lectures, Durham, 1942)
works.form: Three lectures with extensive appendix on the Tao · Anglican Christianity / natural law tradition
works.tagline: Without the natural law, "man's power over nature" becomes some men's power over other men — and the conditioners abolish the very humanity they claim to perfect

works.title: Commentary on Romans (Early)
works.authorDate: Karl Barth · 1919 (1st ed.); 1922 (2nd ed., radically revised)
works.form: Theological commentary on Paul's Epistle to the Romans · Twentieth-century Reformed / dialectical theology
works.tagline: God is in heaven, and you are on the earth — the infinite qualitative difference between God and humanity, recovered against liberal Protestantism

works.title: Physics
works.form: Treatise in eight books · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelian natural philosophy
works.tagline: The four causes, place and void, time as the number of motion — the founding text of Western natural philosophy for two millennia

works.title: Theological-Political Treatise (Early)
works.form: Political-theological treatise in twenty chapters · Early modern rationalism / biblical criticism / political philosophy
works.tagline: Scripture's meaning is to be sought by historical-critical reading; freedom of philosophising is necessary for the state — anonymous and immediately banned

works.title: The Brothers Karamazov (Late)
works.authorDate: Fyodor Dostoevsky · 1879–1880 (serialised in The Russian Messenger)
works.form: Novel in four parts and an epilogue, 12 books · Russian realism / philosophical fiction / Orthodox Christianity
works.tagline: If God does not exist, everything is permitted — and the great theological-philosophical novel that takes the question with full seriousness

works.title: Existentialism Is a Humanism
works.form: Public lecture with question-and-answer transcript · French existentialism
works.tagline: Existence precedes essence — we are nothing until we make ourselves; man is condemned to be free

works.title: Opticks (Late)
works.form: Scientific treatise in three books, with Queries appended · Early modern natural philosophy / experimental method
works.tagline: The corpuscular theory of light, Newton's rings, the spectrum — and the famous Queries that speculatively probe gravity, ether, life, and the nature of God

works.title: Theaetetus (Late)
works.form: Philosophical dialogue · Classical Greek philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: What is knowledge? — three definitions (perception, true belief, true belief with logos) examined and found inadequate; the dialogue ends in aporia

works.title: Crito (Early)
works.authorDate: Plato · c. 399–395 BC (composed shortly after Socrates's death)
works.form: Philosophical dialogue · Classical Greek philosophy / political philosophy
works.tagline: The Laws of Athens speak: by remaining and accepting their benefits, the citizen has tacitly agreed to be bound by them — even unto death

works.title: Laws (Latest)
works.authorDate: Plato · Composed late in life (final years before 347 BC); unrevised at his death
works.form: Three-character dialogue in twelve books · Classical Greek political philosophy / Platonism
works.tagline: Plato's late political philosophy — no longer the philosopher-king but the rule of law, with extensive religious legislation and a Nocturnal Council

works.title: A Letter Concerning Toleration (Late)
works.authorDate: John Locke · Written in Latin 1685 in Holland; published anonymously 1689 (Latin and English)
works.form: Philosophical-political treatise as a letter · Early modern liberal political philosophy
works.tagline: The care of souls cannot belong to the civil magistrate — and toleration of religious difference is the fundamental Christian and civic virtue

works.title: The Natural History of Religion (Late)
works.form: Philosophical-anthropological essay · Scottish Enlightenment / philosophy of religion
works.tagline: Religion arose not from rational argument but from fear of natural events — and polytheism, not monotheism, is the natural human starting-point

works.title: Apologia Pro Vita Sua (Late)
works.authorDate: John Henry Newman · 1864 (in seven weekly instalments)
works.form: Spiritual autobiography · Nineteenth-century English Catholic intellectual revival
works.tagline: Newman's account of his journey from Evangelical Anglican to Catholic — written in seven weeks under attack, becoming one of the great Victorian autobiographies

works.title: On Religion: Speeches to Its Cultured Despisers (Early)
works.authorDate: Friedrich Schleiermacher · 1799 (anonymous first ed.); 1806, 1821, 1831 (revised eds with explanations)
works.form: Five speeches addressed to the Romantic intellectual culture of Berlin · Nineteenth-century liberal Protestant theology / Romantic theology
works.tagline: Religion is neither metaphysics nor morality but the feeling and intuition of the infinite — a defence to Romantic intellectuals who had outgrown orthodox doctrine

works.title: The Cost of Discipleship (Early)
works.form: Theological treatise with extended exposition of Matthew 5–10 · Confessing Church / twentieth-century Lutheran discipleship theology
works.tagline: When Christ calls a man, he bids him come and die — and the distinction between cheap grace and costly grace

works.title: Pragmatism (Late)
works.form: Eight popular philosophical lectures · American pragmatism / Jamesean popular philosophy
works.tagline: Truth happens to an idea — and pragmatism is the philosophical method of evaluating beliefs by their practical consequences

works.title: Political Liberalism (Late)
works.form: Political-philosophical treatise in three parts · Anglo-American liberal political philosophy
works.tagline: How a free society can be just under the permanent fact of reasonable disagreement about comprehensive doctrines — public reason, overlapping consensus, and the political conception of justice

works.title: Two Dogmas of Empiricism
works.form: Philosophical paper, six sections · Analytic philosophy / naturalized epistemology
works.tagline: Two dogmas of empiricism — the analytic/synthetic distinction and reductionism — are untenable; all our beliefs face the tribunal of experience as a corporate body

works.title: Economic and Philosophic Manuscripts of 1844 (Early)
works.authorDate: Karl Marx · Paris, summer 1844 (notebook manuscripts; unfinished and unpublished in Marx's lifetime); first published 1932
works.form: Three philosophical-economic manuscripts in notebook form · Young Marx / philosophical-humanist Marxism
works.tagline: Alienated labour — workers estranged from their products, their activity, their species-being, and each other; the philosophical foundation of mature Marxism

works.title: Beyond Good and Evil (Late)
works.form: Aphoristic philosophical treatise in nine parts · Continental philosophy / mature Nietzsche
works.tagline: Will to power as the basic drive of life — and the philosophers' "free spirits" who have moved beyond the moral dichotomies their tradition imposed

works.title: The Birth of Tragedy (Early)
works.authorDate: Friedrich Nietzsche · 1872 (with "Attempt at a Self-Criticism" preface added 1886)
works.form: Philosophical-philological treatise on Greek tragedy · Continental philosophy / philosophy of art
works.tagline: Apollonian individuation and Dionysian dissolution — and the death of tragedy at the hands of Socratic rationalism

works.title: Cur Deus Homo (Late)
works.form: Two-book dialogue · Medieval Christian theology / Anselmian scholasticism
works.tagline: Sin is an infinite offence against God's honour requiring infinite satisfaction — which only the God-man can offer

works.title: The Ethics of Ambiguity (Early)
works.form: Philosophical-ethical essay · French existentialism / existentialist ethics
works.tagline: The ambiguity of the human condition — radically free yet always situated, oriented to its own freedom yet realised only through commitment to the freedom of others

works.title: The Subjection of Women (Late)
works.authorDate: John Stuart Mill · Written 1860–61 with Harriet Taylor Mill's collaboration; published 1869
works.form: Philosophical-political essay in four chapters · British liberalism / classical feminist liberalism
works.tagline: The legal subordination of one sex to the other is wrong in itself, and one of the chief hindrances to human improvement

works.title: The Rebel (Late)
works.form: Philosophical-political essay · French existentialism / absurdism
works.tagline: I rebel — therefore we exist. The metaphysical and historical analysis of rebellion as the proper response to absurdity, drawing the limits against totalitarian revolution

works.title: The Question Concerning Technology (Late)
works.form: Philosophical lecture · Late Heidegger / continental philosophy of technology
works.tagline: Modern technology's essence is "enframing" — a mode of revealing that reduces all that is to standing-reserve, including humanity itself

works.title: Categories
works.authorDate: Aristotle · c. 350 BC (early in the Aristotelian corpus, opening the Organon)
works.form: Treatise in fifteen chapters · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelian logic
works.tagline: Ten kinds of being — substance, quantity, quality, relation, place, time, position, possession, action, passion — the basic types under which everything that is falls

works.title: The Theory of Moral Sentiments (Early-to-late (he revised it throughout his life))
works.authorDate: Adam Smith · 1759 (1st edition); 1790 (6th and definitive edition with substantial additions)
works.form: Philosophical treatise in seven parts · Scottish Enlightenment / moral sentimentalism
works.tagline: How sympathy and the impartial spectator construct moral judgment — the moral-philosophical foundation underneath the economics of The Wealth of Nations

works.title: Principia Mathematica (Early (both authors))
works.authorDate: Alfred North Whitehead and Bertrand Russell · 1910 (vol. 1), 1912 (vol. 2), 1913 (vol. 3); 2nd edition 1925-27
works.form: Multi-volume formal-logical treatise · Analytic philosophy / philosophy of mathematics / mathematical logic
works.tagline: The logicist programme in its most ambitious form — three volumes deriving arithmetic, set theory, and analysis from a small set of logical primitives

works.title: The Revival of the Religious Sciences (Late (post-crisis))
works.authorDate: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī · c. 1097-1106 (composed during al-Ghazali's years of withdrawal after the 1095 spiritual crisis)
works.form: Forty-book systematic treatise on Islamic spiritual life · Sufi-Sunni Islam / Islamic theology and ethics
works.tagline: Forty books reviving Islamic worship and ethics from the inside out — orthodox law transfigured by Sufi inwardness

works.title: Deliverance from Error (Late)
works.authorDate: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī · c. 1108 (late in al-Ghazali's life, after returning to teaching)
works.form: Spiritual autobiography / philosophical confession · Sufi-Sunni Islam / Islamic philosophical theology
works.tagline: A short philosophical autobiography — doubt undermines kalam, philosophy, and Ismailism in turn, until Sufi experience grants the certainty doctrinal reasoning could not

works.title: On Interpretation
works.authorDate: Aristotle · c. 350 BC (early in the Organon)
works.form: Treatise in fourteen chapters · Classical Greek philosophy / Aristotelian logic
works.tagline: Names, verbs, propositions, opposition, and the famous sea-battle argument that haunted Western philosophy for two thousand years

works.title: Gettysburg Address (Mature (Civil War))
works.authorDate: Abraham Lincoln · November 19, 1863 (delivered 4½ months after the Battle of Gettysburg, July 1–3, 1863)
works.form: Public address — 272 words, ten sentences, c. 2 minutes · American political theology / republican civic religion
works.tagline: "Of the people, by the people, for the people" — the founding text of an American civic religion in which the Civil War becomes a re-founding of the nation on the ground of equality

works.title: Second Inaugural Address (Late (six weeks before assassination))
works.authorDate: Abraham Lincoln · March 4, 1865 (six weeks before his assassination)
works.form: Inaugural address — 700 words, four paragraphs · American political theology / Christian republicanism
works.tagline: "With malice toward none, with charity for all" — Lincoln's most theologically searching speech: the war as divine judgment on both North and South for the offence of slavery

works.title: Whose Justice? Which Rationality? (Mid-late (the second of the After Virtue trilogy))
works.form: Historical-philosophical book in twenty chapters · Contemporary virtue ethics / tradition-constituted rationality
works.tagline: There are no traditionless standards of rationality — only tradition-constituted enquiries. Four traditions of practical rationality, with the Aristotelian-Thomistic the most adequate

works.title: Why I Am Not a Christian (Mid-late)
works.authorDate: Bertrand Russell · 1927 (lecture); 1957 (collected essays as a book)
works.form: Lecture and collected essays · British analytic philosophy / freethought tradition
works.tagline: Russell's atheism in its most popular form — the classical arguments for God dismantled, traditional Christian morality criticised, the religion of free inquiry defended

works.title: The Problem of Pain (Mid (post-conversion, pre-Narnia))
works.form: Theological-philosophical essay in ten chapters · Anglican apologetic / philosophical theology
works.tagline: A theodicy in ten chapters — the goodness and omnipotence of God reconciled with suffering through human fallenness, the necessity of moral freedom, and divine love as deeper than divine kindness

works.title: Discourse on the Method (Mid (1637, in mature middle age; preceding the Meditations of 1641))
works.authorDate: René Descartes · 1637 (published anonymously as the preface to three scientific essays — Optics, Meteorology, Geometry)
works.form: Philosophical autobiography / methodological treatise in six parts · Continental rationalism / early modern philosophy
works.tagline: "I think, therefore I am" — Descartes's first public statement of the cogito and of the method of doubt that founds modern philosophy

works.title: Theodicy (Late)
works.authorDate: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz · 1710 (the only philosophical book Leibniz published in his lifetime)
works.form: Philosophical treatise in three parts, with three preliminary discourses · Continental rationalism / Enlightenment philosophical theology
works.tagline: This is the best of all possible worlds — Leibniz's rationalist theodicy, written for Princess Sophie Charlotte, that gave the genre its name

works.title: Logical Investigations (Early (the breakthrough work that founds phenomenology))
works.authorDate: Edmund Husserl · 1900 (vol. 1, Prolegomena to Pure Logic); 1901 (vol. 2, six investigations); revised editions 1913, 1921
works.form: Two-volume systematic treatise · German phenomenology
works.tagline: The Prolegomena destroys psychologism in logic; the six Investigations begin to lay out the descriptive phenomenology of meaning, perception, and intentional consciousness

works.title: Ideas Pertaining to a Pure Phenomenology (Mid (the transcendental turn))
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in four parts · German phenomenology / transcendental phenomenology
works.tagline: The phenomenological reduction (epoché) and the constitution of the world by transcendental consciousness — Husserl's explicit turn to transcendental idealism

works.title: The Gay Science (Middle (between Daybreak and Zarathustra))
works.authorDate: Friedrich Nietzsche · 1882 (first edition, four books); 1887 (second edition, with added fifth book and preface)
works.form: Aphoristic philosophical book, with verse appendices, in five books · Continental philosophy / existentialism / Nietzschean genealogy
works.tagline: The "death of God" announced; eternal recurrence first formulated; the experimental "gay science" between Daybreak and Zarathustra

works.title: The Concept of Anxiety (Mid (the productive year of 1844 — Concept of Anxiety, Philosophical Fragments, etc.))
works.authorDate: Søren Kierkegaard · 1844 (published under the pseudonym Vigilius Haufniensis)
works.form: Pseudonymous theological-psychological treatise in five chapters · Danish religious existentialism
works.tagline: Anxiety as "the dizziness of freedom" — the existential-theological analysis of the human condition between possibility and actuality

works.title: A Treatise Concerning Religious Affections (Mid (Northampton pastorate; the major work of evangelical reflection))
works.form: Theological treatise in three parts · American Reformed-Calvinist evangelical theology
works.tagline: True religion consists chiefly in holy affections — Edwards's twelve signs distinguishing genuine spiritual experience from its counterfeits

works.title: The History of Sexuality (Late (his last major project))
works.authorDate: Michel Foucault · 1976 (vol. 1); 1984 (vols. 2-3, shortly before Foucault's death); vol. 4 (Confessions of the Flesh) published posthumously 2018
works.form: Multi-volume historical-philosophical study · French postmodernism / Foucauldian genealogy
works.tagline: The "repressive hypothesis" inverted: sexuality is not what power represses but what power produces — and the genealogy of the modern sexual subject through Greek, Roman, and early Christian practices of the self

works.title: Divine Comedy: Inferno (Late (Dante's exile years))
works.authorDate: Dante Alighieri · c. 1308-1320 (composed during Dante's exile from Florence; completed shortly before his death in 1321)
works.form: Epic poem in 34 canti of terza rima · Medieval Italian Christian literature / Scholastic theological allegory
works.tagline: "In the middle of the journey of our life" — Dante's descent through nine circles of Hell, guided by Virgil, in the first canticle of the Divine Comedy

works.title: A Plain Account of Christian Perfection (Late)
works.authorDate: John Wesley · 1766 (with revisions through 1777; published as a unified text in 1777)
works.form: Theological autobiography / doctrinal synthesis · English Methodist / Wesleyan-Arminian evangelical
works.tagline: Wesley's defining doctrine — the Christian's call to entire sanctification or "perfect love" in this present life

works.title: I and Thou (Mid (the foundational statement of dialogical philosophy))
works.form: Philosophical-theological essay in three parts · Jewish religious philosophy / dialogical philosophy
works.tagline: The two basic word-pairs — I-Thou and I-It — and the eternal Thou that meets us through every finite Thou

works.title: Eichmann in Jerusalem: A Report on the Banality of Evil (Mid-late (after The Human Condition, before The Life of the Mind))
works.authorDate: Hannah Arendt · 1963 (New Yorker articles 1962-63, then book)
works.form: Long-form journalism / philosophical reportage in fifteen chapters · Twentieth-century political philosophy / Jewish thought
works.tagline: The "banality of evil" — Eichmann not as a monster but as a bureaucratically thoughtless functionary, raising the most disturbing question of twentieth-century ethics

works.title: Walden (Mid (Thoreau's major prose statement))
works.authorDate: Henry David Thoreau · 1854 (drawing on Thoreau's 1845-47 residence at Walden Pond)
works.form: Philosophical-naturalistic narrative in eighteen chapters · American transcendentalism / nature writing
works.tagline: "I went to the woods because I wished to live deliberately" — Thoreau's two-year experiment in simple living and his philosophical-naturalistic reflection on its meaning

works.title: Elements of the Philosophy of Right (Late (the mature systematic philosophy))
works.authorDate: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel · 1820 (published 1821 with the famous controversial Preface)
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in three parts · German idealism / political philosophy
works.tagline: The dialectical unfolding of freedom in abstract right, morality, and ethical life — culminating in the rational state. "The rational is actual and the actual is rational"

works.title: Philosophy and the Mirror of Nature (Mid (the breakthrough book))
works.form: Philosophical-historical book in three parts · American neo-pragmatism / postanalytic philosophy
works.tagline: The mind as the mirror of nature — the founding image of modern epistemology — historically deconstructed. After foundationalism, edifying philosophical conversation

works.title: Works of Love (Late (after the pseudonymous works; the major direct theological work))
works.authorDate: Søren Kierkegaard · 1847 (published under his own name, not pseudonymous)
works.form: Theological-philosophical meditation in two series · Danish religious existentialism
works.tagline: "You shall love your neighbour" — Kierkegaard's sustained meditation on Christian love as commanded, neighbourly, and concretely actualised

works.title: Essays: First Series (Mid (Emerson at the peak of his powers))
works.authorDate: Ralph Waldo Emerson · 1841 (twelve essays collected from earlier lectures and journal entries)
works.form: Twelve philosophical essays · American transcendentalism
works.tagline: "Trust thyself: every heart vibrates to that iron string" — Emerson's essays on the self, the Over-Soul, friendship, love, prudence, heroism, and the moral order

works.title: The Will to Believe (Mid (between Principles of Psychology and Varieties of Religious Experience))
works.authorDate: William James · 1897 (title essay, addressed to the Philosophical Clubs of Yale and Brown, 1896)
works.form: Collection of ten popular philosophical essays · American pragmatism / philosophy of religion
works.tagline: In genuine options that cannot be settled on intellectual grounds, our passional and volitional nature must decide — and may legitimately decide for religious belief

works.title: The Nature and Destiny of Man (Mid-late (Niebuhr's major systematic work))
works.authorDate: Reinhold Niebuhr · 1941 (vol. I, Human Nature); 1943 (vol. II, Human Destiny) — based on the Gifford Lectures, Edinburgh, 1939
works.form: Two-volume theological-philosophical treatise · American mainline Protestant Christian realism
works.tagline: Christian anthropology and eschatology against modern optimism — human nature as the paradoxical synthesis of nature and spirit, sin and grace

works.title: Adventures of Ideas (Late (Whitehead's last major book))
works.form: Philosophical-historical study in four parts · Process philosophy / philosophy of culture
works.tagline: The civilising adventure of ideas — humanity, freedom, persuasion replacing coercion, beauty and truth and peace as the highest civilisational goods

works.title: The Origins of Totalitarianism (Mid (Arendt's breakthrough book))
works.authorDate: Hannah Arendt · 1951 (with later editions adding new prefaces and material through 1968)
works.form: Three-part historical-political study · Twentieth-century political philosophy
works.tagline: Antisemitism, imperialism, totalitarianism — Arendt's 1951 study of how Europe arrived at the death camps and the Gulag

works.title: On Revolution (Late (after Eichmann in Jerusalem))
works.form: Political-historical study in six chapters · Twentieth-century political philosophy
works.tagline: The lost treasure of revolution — public freedom rediscovered, then forgotten. The American success and the French failure

works.title: Dependent Rational Animals (Late (the explicit Thomist completion of the After Virtue trilogy))
works.form: Philosophical-anthropological treatise in nine chapters · Contemporary virtue ethics / philosophical anthropology
works.tagline: Humans as rational dependent animals — vulnerability and disability not as exceptions but as central to ethical-political reflection. The virtues of acknowledged dependence

works.title: Life Together (Mid (between the Cost of Discipleship and the prison theology))
works.authorDate: Dietrich Bonhoeffer · 1939 (drawn from the Finkenwalde seminary, 1935-37)
works.form: Short theological treatise in five chapters · German Lutheran-evangelical / Confessing Church
works.tagline: Christian community as gift, not ideal — Bonhoeffer's short and influential guide to common life, drawn from the underground Confessing seminary at Finkenwalde

works.title: The Copernican Revolution (Early (Kuhn's first book))
works.form: Historical-philosophical study in seven chapters · History and philosophy of science
works.tagline: Astronomy from antiquity to Newton, traced as a developmental whole — the historical study that gave Kuhn the questions answered five years later in Structure

works.title: The Sovereignty of Good (Mid (her major philosophical statement, alongside Metaphysics as a Guide to Morals 1992))
works.form: Collection of three philosophical essays · Twentieth-century British moral philosophy / Platonic-Christian moral realism
works.tagline: Moral life as attention to the real — Murdoch's recovery of moral realism against existentialist and analytic reductions of ethics to choice

works.title: Science of Logic (Mid (the central work of the mature Hegelian system))
works.authorDate: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel · 1812 (Book I, Being); 1813 (Book II, Essence); 1816 (Book III, Concept); 1832 (Hegel's revised Book I, posthumous)
works.form: Multi-volume systematic philosophical treatise · German absolute idealism
works.tagline: The dialectical self-movement of pure concepts — Being passing into Nothing, Essence unfolding into Concept. Hegel's most ambitious and most difficult book

works.title: The Structural Transformation of the Public Sphere (Early (the breakthrough work))
works.form: Historical-sociological-philosophical study · Frankfurt School / critical theory
works.tagline: The historical emergence and modern decline of the bourgeois public sphere — the locus of rational-critical discussion that grounded liberal democracy

works.title: The Epistle to the Romans (Early (the breakthrough work))
works.authorDate: Karl Barth · 1919 (first edition); 1922 (second edition — the famous and influential one, almost completely rewritten)
works.form: Theological commentary on Paul's Epistle to the Romans · Twentieth-century dialectical theology / Reformed neo-orthodoxy
works.tagline: The infinite qualitative distinction between God and humanity — Barth's break with liberal Protestantism and the founding of dialectical theology

works.title: The Origin of German Tragic Drama (Early (the most ambitious early work, before the Arcades Project))
works.authorDate: Walter Benjamin · 1925 (submitted as habilitation thesis, rejected by the University of Frankfurt); 1928 (published commercially)
works.form: Philosophical-literary historical study with extensive epistemological-critical Preface · German critical theory / philosophy of history
works.tagline: The German Baroque Trauerspiel and the philosophical theory of allegory — Benjamin's rejected habilitation thesis, now recognised as a masterpiece of critical theory

works.title: The Social Contract (Late (after the two Discourses; the political conclusion of Rousseau's mature thought))
works.form: Political treatise in four books · Enlightenment political philosophy / republican theory
works.tagline: "Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains" — Rousseau's account of legitimate authority as grounded in the general will of the people

works.title: Émile (Late)
works.authorDate: Jean-Jacques Rousseau · 1762 (published the same year as the Social Contract; both condemned and burned by authorities)
works.form: Philosophical-educational treatise in five books · Enlightenment educational and moral philosophy
works.tagline: "Everything is good as it leaves the hands of the Author of things; everything degenerates in the hands of man" — Rousseau's landmark treatise on education according to nature

works.title: Discourse on the Origin of Inequality (Mid (between the First Discourse and the Social Contract))
works.authorDate: Jean-Jacques Rousseau · 1755 (submitted to the 1754 essay competition of the Académie de Dijon, on the question of the origin and justification of inequality)
works.form: Philosophical-historical essay in two parts, with preface · Enlightenment political-philosophical anthropology
works.tagline: "The first man who, having enclosed a piece of land, said 'this is mine'..." — Rousseau's philosophical anthropology of the conjectural state of nature and the historical genesis of social inequality

works.title: The Principles of Psychology (Mid (the major early work; foundational for both psychology and pragmatist philosophy))
works.authorDate: William James · 1890 (after twelve years of writing; James later said he should not have spent so much time on it)
works.form: Two-volume systematic treatise in twenty-eight chapters · American pragmatism / scientific psychology / philosophy of mind
works.tagline: The stream of thought, the will, habit, emotion, attention — James's 1890 founding synthesis of scientific psychology with philosophical anthropology

works.title: Monologion (Early (Anselm's first major work, before the Proslogion))
works.authorDate: Anselm of Canterbury · c. 1076 (composed at the abbey of Bec; the first major work of mature scholastic theology)
works.form: Monologue / philosophical-theological meditation in 80 short chapters · Medieval Christian scholastic theology
works.tagline: A meditation on the divine nature reasoned from rational principles alone — the seedbed for the more famous Proslogion

works.title: Otherwise than Being (Late (the more radical successor to Totality and Infinity, 1961))
works.form: Phenomenological-philosophical treatise · French phenomenology / Jewish religious philosophy
works.tagline: Beyond being — substitution, the trace, the saying and the said. Levinas's 1974 attempt to articulate ethical responsibility in a register beyond ontology

works.title: The Logical Structure of the World (Early (Carnap's breakthrough work))
works.authorDate: Rudolf Carnap · 1928 (Carnap's habilitation; the founding text of the Vienna Circle's constructive-philosophical programme)
works.form: Systematic philosophical-logical treatise · Logical positivism / Vienna Circle
works.tagline: All empirical concepts constructed from immediate experience using the logical apparatus of Principia Mathematica — Carnap's most ambitious early book

works.title: Zhuangzi
works.authorDate: Zhuangzi (Zhuang Zhou) · c. 4th-3rd c. BC (Inner Chapters by Zhuang Zhou; Outer and Miscellaneous Chapters by later hands)
works.form: Anthology of philosophical-literary chapters in three sections (Inner, Outer, Miscellaneous) · Classical Daoism
works.tagline: The butterfly dream, the cook cutting the ox, the useless tree — Zhuangzi's playful-philosophical parables on the Dao, perspective, and the freedom of wu wei

works.title: The Great Learning and Doctrine of the Mean
works.authorDate: Confucius (Kongzi) · Originally chapters of the Book of Rites (Li Ji, c. 1st c. BC); elevated to the Four Books by Zhu Xi (1130-1200) in the Song dynasty
works.form: Two short philosophical treatises · Classical Confucianism / Neo-Confucianism
works.tagline: The eight steps of self-cultivation (Da Xue) and the metaphysics of sincerity and the mean (Zhong Yong) — two short Confucian classics that became foundational for neo-Confucian thought

works.title: Enchiridion Militis Christiani (Early (Erasmus's first major work))
works.authorDate: Desiderius Erasmus of Rotterdam · 1503 (with a famous expanded 1518 preface that became a humanist-Reformation manifesto)
works.form: Spiritual-philosophical handbook in twenty-two chapters · Renaissance Christian humanism / Northern Renaissance reform
works.tagline: "The Christian Soldier's Handbook" — Erasmus's 1503 manual of interior Christianity, the founding text of Christian humanism and a major source for the Reformation

works.title: The Stranger (Early (the breakthrough novel))
works.authorDate: Albert Camus · 1942 (alongside The Myth of Sisyphus; published in occupied Paris)
works.form: Short novel in two parts · French absurdism / mid-century existentialist literature
works.tagline: "Maman died today. Or maybe yesterday" — Meursault's flat-affect narration through accidental murder to execution, the literary embodiment of Camus's philosophy of the absurd

works.title: The Plague (Mid (between The Stranger and The Rebel))
works.form: Novel in five parts · French absurdism / post-war existentialist literature
works.tagline: A plague outbreak in the Algerian city of Oran — allegory of Nazi occupation, meditation on solidarity under absurdity. "There are more things to admire in men than to despise"

works.title: Brave New World (Mid (Huxley's breakthrough novel))
works.form: Dystopian novel · Twentieth-century English dystopian literature / philosophy of technology
works.tagline: The World State of pleasant conditioning, soma, and engineered happiness — Huxley's 1932 dystopia of total social engineering, against which the Savage rebels

works.title: The Perennial Philosophy (Late (Huxley's mature spiritual-philosophical synthesis))
works.form: Anthology with commentary, in twenty-seven chapters · Twentieth-century perennialism / comparative mysticism
works.tagline: The common metaphysical-experiential core of the world's mystical traditions — Christian, Hindu, Buddhist, Sufi, Taoist — as a single perennial philosophy

works.title: The Doors of Perception (Late)
works.authorDate: Aldous Huxley · 1954 (essay-length; often published together with the 1956 Heaven and Hell)
works.form: Long philosophical essay · Twentieth-century psychedelic-philosophical writing
works.tagline: "If the doors of perception were cleansed" — Huxley's 1954 essay on his first mescaline experience, the founding text of modern psychedelic-philosophical writing

works.title: Ideas and Opinions (Late (the most comprehensive single-volume collection))
works.authorDate: Albert Einstein · 1954 (collected from earlier essays and addresses)
works.form: Collection of essays and addresses, organised thematically · Twentieth-century philosophy of science / public-philosophical reflection
works.tagline: Einstein on science, religion, ethics, politics — the major collection of his philosophical and public reflections

works.title: Science and the Modern World (Mid (the major statement of philosophical-cultural critique, preceding the technical metaphysics of Process and Reality))
works.authorDate: Alfred North Whitehead · 1925 (the Lowell Lectures, Harvard; the proximate prelude to Process and Reality, 1929)
works.form: Lectures in thirteen chapters · Process philosophy / philosophy of science
works.tagline: The "fallacy of misplaced concreteness" — Whitehead's critique of scientific materialism and the proximate prelude to process philosophy
