works.authorDate: Huineng (attributed; compiled by disciples) · c. 780 (earliest Dunhuang manuscript; text attributed to Huineng, d. 713)
works.form: Sermon collection and spiritual autobiography · Chan (Zen) Buddhism
works.tagline: "From the beginning not a thing is" — the Sixth Patriarch's teaching of sudden awakening, no-mind, and the Buddha-nature present in all beings

works.title: De Rerum Naturis
works.form: Encyclopedia in twenty-two books · Carolingian Latin encyclopedism
works.tagline: Every creature a sign of God — the Carolingian encyclopedia that made the natural world a book of divine instruction

works.title: Kontakia (Selected Hymns)
works.authorDate: Romanos the Melodist · c. 520–555 (during the reign of Justinian I)
works.form: Kontakia — long metrical hymns with prooimion, strophes (oikoi), and refrain · Byzantine liturgical hymnography
works.tagline: The Virgin weeps, Judas speaks, the cave receives the Infinite — theology as dramatic song in the liturgical assembly

works.title: Bibliotheca (Myriobiblon)
works.authorDate: Photius I of Constantinople · c. 845–855 (before Photius became patriarch)
works.form: Bibliographic compilation of 280 codices (book reviews/summaries) · Byzantine classical scholarship and literary criticism
works.tagline: The library of a lost world — 280 book reviews that preserve the memory of classical Greek literature otherwise vanished

works.title: Penitential and Monastic Rules
works.authorDate: Columbanus · c. 590–615 (composed during his continental mission)
works.form: Penitential code and two monastic rules (Regula Monachorum and Regula Coenobialis) · Irish monastic and penitential literature
works.tagline: A tariff for the soul — the Irish monk's system of private confession and calibrated penance that became the ancestor of the modern confessional

works.title: Memorial on the Bone of the Buddha
works.form: Memorial (shangshu/biao) addressed to Emperor Xianzong · Confucian political remonstrance
works.tagline: Since Buddhism entered China the dynasties have been short-lived — the fearless memorial that told the emperor to burn the bone and restore the ancient Way

works.title: Seventeen-Article Constitution
works.form: Seventeen articles of moral-political injunction · Japanese Asuka-period statecraft; Buddhist-Confucian political synthesis
works.tagline: Harmony is to be valued — seventeen articles that made Buddhism and Confucianism the moral foundations of the Japanese state

works.title: Definitions of Philosophy
works.authorDate: David the Invincible · c. 5th–6th century (precise date uncertain)
works.form: Philosophical prolegomenon (introduction to philosophy) · Alexandrian Neoplatonist commentary tradition
works.tagline: What is philosophy? — six answers from the Greek tradition harmonised into a single propaedeutic for the Armenian philosophical curriculum

works.title: Ecclesiastes (Qohelet)
works.authorDate: Solomon (traditional attribution); anonymous sage (scholarly consensus: c. 3rd century BCE) · c. 3rd century BCE (traditionally attributed to 10th century BCE)
works.form: Wisdom literature — meditative reflections and proverbs · Israelite wisdom tradition
works.tagline: Vanity of vanities — all is vanity; the most searching meditation on meaninglessness in the Hebrew Bible

works.title: Book of Isaiah (chapters 1-39)
works.authorDate: Isaiah ben Amoz · c. 740–700 BCE (oracles); redacted and compiled later
works.form: Prophetic oracles, vision narratives, historical prose · Israelite prophetic tradition
works.tagline: Holy, holy, holy — the prophet who saw God high and lifted up and demanded justice for the oppressed

works.title: Book of Jeremiah
works.authorDate: Jeremiah ben Hilkiah (with Baruch ben Neriah as scribe) · c. 627–580 BCE (oracles); redacted and expanded through the 6th–5th centuries BCE
works.form: Prophetic oracles, biographical prose, confessional poetry, historical narrative · Israelite prophetic tradition
works.tagline: The suffering prophet who promised a new covenant written on the heart — from the ruins of Jerusalem

works.title: Book of Ezekiel
works.authorDate: Ezekiel ben Buzi · c. 593–571 BCE (oracles); compiled and edited in the exilic and early post-exilic period
works.form: Prophetic oracles, vision narratives, symbolic actions, temple blueprint · Israelite prophetic and priestly tradition
works.tagline: The glory departs and the glory returns — exile, resurrection, and the new temple in visionary geometry

works.title: On Nature (fragments)
works.authorDate: Anaximenes of Miletus · c. mid-6th century BCE
works.form: Philosophical prose treatise (fragments and testimonia) · Pre-Socratic natural philosophy / Milesian school
works.tagline: Air as arche — rarefaction yields fire, condensation yields wind, cloud, water, earth, stone: the first quantitative mechanism of change

works.title: On Floating Bodies
works.form: Mathematical-physical treatise (axioms, propositions, proofs) · Greek mathematical physics
works.tagline: A body immersed in fluid is buoyed up by a force equal to the weight of the fluid displaced — the birth of mathematical physics

works.title: On the Measurement of the Earth (reconstructed)
works.form: Scientific treatise (reconstructed from secondary sources) · Hellenistic mathematical geography and astronomy
works.tagline: Shadow, distance, geometry — and the size of the world is known: the first precision measurement of the Earth

works.title: Conics
works.form: Mathematical treatise (definitions, propositions, proofs) · Greek higher geometry
works.tagline: Parabola, ellipse, hyperbola — the curves that would become the orbits of planets, defined here in pure geometry

works.title: Lectures and Sayings
works.authorDate: Gaius Musonius Rufus · c. 60–100 CE (lectures delivered); excerpts preserved 5th c. CE
works.form: Diatribes (lecture-discourses) and aphoristic excerpts · Roman Stoicism
works.tagline: The Roman Socrates on practical ethics — women deserve philosophy, food should be simple, exile is no evil, and virtue is practised, not theorised

works.title: Discourses (Orations)
works.authorDate: Dio Chrysostom (Dio of Prusa) · c. 70–115 CE
works.form: Orations (speeches) and diatribes · Second Sophistic; Stoic-Cynic popular philosophy
works.tagline: Eighty orations from the golden-mouthed wandering sage — kingship, cosmology, the simple life, and philosophy as civic duty

works.title: Letters (fragments) and Testimonia
works.authorDate: Apollonius of Tyana · c. 1st century CE (letters); testimonia collected 2nd–4th c. CE
works.form: Epistolary fragments and biographical testimonia · Neo-Pythagoreanism; late antique pagan piety
works.tagline: The voice of the wandering sage — Pythagorean piety, cosmic sympathy, and the reform of worship, surviving in fragments and the memories of later writers

works.title: Elements of Theology
works.form: Axiomatic philosophical treatise (propositions with proofs) · Neoplatonism; Athenian Academy
works.tagline: 211 propositions from the One to matter — the architecture of emanation, procession, remaining, and reversion in deductive form

works.title: On Dreams (De Insomniis)
works.form: Philosophical treatise · Alexandrian Neoplatonism; Christian Platonism
works.tagline: Dreaming as philosophy — the imaginative spirit as gateway between body and intellect, cosmic sympathy as the medium of nocturnal revelation

works.title: Tome of Leo (Epistola XXVIII)
works.authorDate: Pope Leo I (Leo the Great) · 449 CE
works.form: Theological epistle · Latin patristic theology; Chalcedonian Christianity
works.tagline: "Peter has spoken through Leo" — the Christological formula of two complete natures in one person, each acting according to its proper character

works.title: Confessio
works.form: Spiritual autobiography and apologia · Latin patristic Christianity; insular Christianity
works.tagline: "I am Patrick, a sinner, most unlearned" — Providence working through weakness, captivity turned to mission, faith as the answer to exile

works.title: The Marriage of Philology and Mercury
works.form: Prosimetrum (alternating prose and verse); encyclopedic allegory · Late Latin encyclopedism; Neoplatonic-Stoic syncretism
works.tagline: The cosmic wedding that shaped a thousand years of education — Grammar, Dialectic, Rhetoric, Geometry, Arithmetic, Astronomy, and Music as the seven bridesmaids

works.title: Platonic Theology (Mature (Ficino's philosophical magnum opus, composed during the height of his work at the Florentine Academy))
works.form: Systematic philosophical treatise in 18 books · Renaissance Christian Platonism / Neoplatonism / prisca theologia
works.tagline: The soul is the copula mundi — the knot binding divine and corporeal, immortal by its own nature as the Platonic tradition demonstrates and Christian faith confirms

works.title: Oration on the Dignity of Man (Early (Pico was 23 years old; this was his first major philosophical statement))
works.authorDate: Giovanni Pico della Mirandola · 1486 (composed as the opening address for the planned Roman disputation of the 900 Theses; the disputation never took place)
works.form: Oration (philosophical address) · Renaissance humanism / Christian Platonism / prisca theologia / syncretism
works.tagline: God gave man no fixed nature — placed at the centre of creation, he can freely fashion himself as beast or angel

works.title: Notebooks (Codex Atlanticus and others) (Lifelong (the notebooks span Leonardo's entire adult career))
works.authorDate: Leonardo da Vinci · c. 1478–1519 (across Leonardo's entire career, from Florence through Milan, Rome, and Amboise)
works.form: Private notebooks, sketches, and treatise drafts · Renaissance natural philosophy / empirical investigation / art-science synthesis
works.tagline: Experience is the mother of all certainty — the greatest Renaissance mind's direct encounter with anatomy, mechanics, optics, and the natural world

works.title: The Prince (Mature (Machiavelli was 44 and writing from the bitter experience of political defeat and exile))
works.authorDate: Niccolo Machiavelli · 1513 (composed in exile at Sant'Andrea in Percussina; published posthumously, 1532)
works.form: Political treatise in 26 chapters · Renaissance political thought / political realism / mirror of princes (inverted)
works.tagline: The effective truth of the thing — how states are actually maintained, not how one might wish they were

works.title: On the Differences between Plato and Aristotle (Late (Pletho was approximately 84; the work is the product of a lifetime of Platonist conviction))
works.authorDate: Georgius Gemistus Pletho · c. 1439 (composed in connection with Pletho's attendance at the Council of Florence)
works.form: Philosophical treatise / polemical comparison · Byzantine Platonism / Renaissance Plato-Aristotle controversy
works.tagline: Plato is superior to Aristotle in theology, ethics, and metaphysics — and the Latin West has been led astray by preferring the student to the master

works.title: Gutenberg Bible (Mature (the culmination of approximately 15 years of experimentation with movable type))
works.authorDate: Johannes Gutenberg (Johannes Gensfleisch zur Laden) · c. 1452–1455 (printed in Mainz; approximately 180 copies produced)
works.form: Printed book (two volumes, 42 lines per page) · Technological-cultural artifact / Vulgate biblical tradition / European print revolution
works.tagline: The technology that broke the manuscript monopoly — 180 identical Bibles produced in the time it would have taken a scribe to copy one

works.title: Bijak (Lifelong (the poems represent Kabir's entire career; the collection is posthumous))
works.authorDate: Kabir · c. 15th century (oral composition across Kabir's lifetime; collected and written down by disciples; the Bijak as a text dates from the 17th century)
works.form: Collection of poems, songs (pads), and couplets (sakhis) · North Indian bhakti / Sufi mysticism / Sant tradition
works.tagline: Neither Hindu nor Muslim — the fish in the water is thirsty: the divine is found within, and the seekers who look elsewhere are looking in the wrong direction

works.title: Book of Amos
works.form: Prophetic oracle collection (9 chapters) · Israelite prophetic literature
works.tagline: Let justice roll down like waters — the first literary prophet condemns a prosperous nation for grinding the poor

works.title: Book of Hosea
works.form: Prophetic oracle collection (14 chapters) · Israelite prophetic literature
works.tagline: I desire steadfast love and not sacrifice — the covenant as marriage, betrayal as adultery, restoration as remarriage

works.title: Poems and Laws (fragments)
works.form: Elegiac and iambic verse fragments; law code (attested in later sources) · Archaic Greek lyric poetry / Athenian legal tradition
works.tagline: Eunomia or dysnomia — the citizen-poet argues that only justice can save the city from itself

works.title: Fragments and Testimonia
works.authorDate: Thespis of Icaria · c. 534 BCE onward
works.form: Dramatic fragments and ancient testimonia · Archaic Greek tragedy / Dionysiac festival
works.tagline: The first actor steps out of the chorus — ritual becomes theatre, and a new way of knowing the human condition is born

works.title: Fragments (Silloi and On Nature)
works.form: Satirical elegies (Silloi) and didactic hexameter (On Nature) — fragmentary · Pre-Socratic Greek philosophy / Ionian natural theology
works.tagline: If horses had gods they would look like horses — the first systematic critique of anthropomorphic religion

works.title: On Nature (fragments)
works.authorDate: Alcmaeon of Croton · c. 5th century BCE
works.form: Prose treatise (fragmentary — surviving in Aristotle, Theophrastus, Aëtius) · Pre-Socratic natural philosophy / early Greek medicine
works.tagline: The brain is the seat of thought and health is the balance of opposing powers — empirical medicine meets Pythagorean cosmology

works.title: On Nature (fragments)
works.form: Prose treatise (fragmentary — surviving in Stobaeus, Diogenes Laertius, Nicomachus, Iamblichus) · Pythagorean cosmology / pre-Socratic philosophy
works.tagline: Nature was fitted together from unlimiteds and limiters — the first mathematical cosmology, with the Earth in motion

works.title: Fragments and Testimonia
works.form: Prose treatises (fragmentary — surviving in Aristotle, Proclus, Iamblichus, Athenaeus) · Academic Platonism / Pythagorean-Platonic synthesis
works.tagline: The One is not the Good — mathematical entities, not transcendent Forms, are the primary realities

works.title: Natural History
works.form: Encyclopedia (37 books) · Roman encyclopedic naturalism
works.tagline: Nature is to be found in her entirety nowhere more than in her smallest creations — the ancient world catalogued

works.title: Institutio Oratoria
works.form: Pedagogical treatise (12 books) · Roman rhetorical tradition
works.tagline: The good man speaking well — a complete programme of education from the nursery to the forum

works.title: Geography
works.form: Geographical treatise (17 books) · Hellenistic geography and Stoic philosophy
works.tagline: Geography as the philosopher's discipline — the known world surveyed with Stoic cosmology and empirical rigour

works.title: Satyricon
works.form: Prose fiction with verse inserts (Menippean satire / novel) · Roman satire and the ancient novel
works.tagline: Trimalchio's feast as the mirror of empire — a picaresque masterpiece of social comedy and moral nihilism

works.title: De Architectura
works.form: Technical treatise (10 books) · Roman architectural and engineering tradition
works.tagline: Firmitas, utilitas, venustas — the only surviving ancient treatise on architecture and the origin of the Vitruvian Man

works.title: Elements
works.form: Mathematical treatise (13 books) · Greek axiomatic mathematics
works.tagline: There is no royal road to geometry — the most successful textbook in the history of mathematics

works.title: On the Sizes and Distances of the Sun and Moon
works.form: Geometric-astronomical treatise · Greek mathematical astronomy
works.tagline: The sun is far larger than the earth — the geometric proof that made heliocentrism thinkable

works.title: On Pneumatics
works.form: Technical treatise (surviving only in secondary descriptions) · Alexandrian engineering and applied mathematics
works.tagline: The father of pneumatics — compressed air, flowing water, and the birth of experimental engineering

works.title: Rule of St Benedict
works.form: Monastic rule (73 chapters of practical and spiritual legislation) · Catholic / Western monastic
works.tagline: A school for the Lord's service — ora et labora as the balanced path to God

works.title: Against the Nestorians and Eutychians
works.form: Theological treatise in three books · Eastern Orthodox / Chalcedonian
works.tagline: Enhypostasia — a nature subsists within a hypostasis, solving the riddle of Christ's two-natured unity

works.title: Nahj al-Balagha
works.authorDate: Ali ibn Abi Talib (compiled by al-Sharif al-Radi, d. 1015) · c. 7th century CE (compiled c. 1010 CE)
works.form: Collection of sermons (khutab), letters (kutub), and sayings (hikam) · Islamic (Shia and Sunni)
works.tagline: The Peak of Eloquence — theology, governance, and wisdom from the gate of prophetic knowledge

works.title: History of the Franks
works.form: Prose history in ten books · Catholic / Merovingian Gaul
works.tagline: Providential history — God's judgement and mercy in the rise and fall of Merovingian kings

works.title: Letter on Qadar
works.form: Theological epistle (risala) addressed to Caliph Abd al-Malik · Islamic (early Kalam / proto-Mu'tazili)
works.tagline: God does not compel sin — the earliest Islamic argument for human moral freedom against fatalism

works.title: Legal and Theological Teachings of Ja'far al-Sadiq
works.authorDate: Ja'far al-Sadiq (compiled by al-Kulayni, al-Saduq, and others) · c. 720–765 CE (compiled 9th–10th century)
works.form: Hadith reports (akhbar) covering jurisprudence, theology, ethics, and exegesis · Islamic (Shia / Ja'fari)
works.tagline: The Truthful Imam's legacy — jurisprudence, theology, and esoteric knowledge from the Prophet's lineage

works.title: Commentary on the Awakening of Mahayana Faith
works.form: Exegetical commentary (so) on the Awakening of Faith treatise · Mahayana Buddhist (Korean / East Asian)
works.tagline: One Mind, two aspects — tathagatagarbha and alayavijnana are not two but one, and all beings can awaken

works.title: Vexilla Regis and Selected Hymns
works.form: Liturgical hymns (iambic dimeter and trochaic tetrameter) · Catholic / Western liturgical
works.tagline: Vexilla Regis prodeunt — the Cross as royal banner, where classical verse meets Christian mystery

works.title: Meadows of Gold and Mines of Gems
works.form: Encyclopaedic universal history and geography (30+ volumes) · Islamic historiography and geography
works.tagline: The Herodotus of the Arabs surveys every civilisation he can reach — universal history grounded in the traveller's own eyes

works.title: Shahnameh (Book of Kings)
works.form: Epic poem in masnavi couplets (c. 50,000 couplets) · Persian epic and Zoroastrian mythological tradition
works.tagline: I revived the Persians with this Persian language — fifty thousand couplets against the erasure of a civilisation

works.title: Hymns of Divine Love
works.authorDate: Symeon the New Theologian · c. 1000–1022 CE
works.form: Lyric hymns in free verse and political verse (58 hymns) · Byzantine Orthodox mystical theology
works.tagline: I have seen the Light — the uncreated fire breaks into the monk's cell and transforms the body itself

works.title: Letters and Scientific Writings
works.authorDate: Gerbert of Aurillac (Pope Sylvester II) · c. 980–1003 CE
works.form: Epistolary collection, mathematical treatises, and astronomical descriptions · Latin Carolingian-Ottonian scholarship
works.tagline: The abacus, the astrolabe, and Arabic numerals — the first mediaeval scholar-pope transmits Islamic science to Latin Christendom

works.title: Best Divisions for Knowledge of the Regions
works.form: Descriptive geography (14 regional divisions) · Islamic geography (Balkhi school)
works.tagline: I have not described what I have not seen — the fourteen regions of the Islamic world through the geographer's own eyes

works.title: Fragments
works.form: Lyric, iambic, and elegiac fragments (c. 300 surviving fragments) · Greek archaic lyric and iambic poetry
works.tagline: Some Thracian delights in my shield — the first-person singular erupts into Western literature

works.title: Fragments (War Elegies)
works.form: Elegiac couplets (fragments) · Greek martial elegy
works.tagline: It is a beautiful thing to die in the front ranks — the Spartan war-poet redefines arete as civic sacrifice

works.title: Fragments
works.form: Lyric stanzas in Alcaic and other metres (fragments) · Greek archaic Aeolian lyric
works.tagline: The ship of state is driven by the storm — wine, war, and exile in the world's first political lyric

works.title: Attributed Sayings and Wisdom Traditions
works.authorDate: Imhotep · c. 2650–2600 BCE (original period; surviving references from later periods)
works.form: Wisdom sayings, medical case reports, and architectural principles (reconstructed from later references) · Egyptian wisdom literature (sebayt)
works.tagline: The lost voice of the first sage — wisdom, healing, and sacred architecture at the dawn of civilisation

works.title: The Exaltation of Inanna (Nin-me-šara)
works.form: Hymn (153 lines on cuneiform tablets) · Sumerian temple hymn literature
works.tagline: "Lady of all the me" — the first named author's cosmic hymn, personal lament, and theological revolution in a single voice

works.title: Code of Hammurabi
works.form: Legal code inscribed on a diorite stele (prologue, 282 laws, epilogue) · Mesopotamian legal tradition
works.tagline: "An eye for an eye" — the first great code of written law, balancing divine justice, royal authority, and social hierarchy

works.title: Great Hymn to the Aten
works.form: Hymn (inscribed in the tomb of Ay at Amarna) · Egyptian religious literature / Amarna theology
works.tagline: "O sole god, like whom there is no other" — the first monotheistic hymn, celebrating the Aten as universal creator and source of all life

works.title: Torah (Pentateuch)
works.authorDate: Moses (traditional attribution) · c. 13th–5th century BCE (traditional: c. 1400–1200 BCE; critical: redacted over centuries)
works.form: Narrative, law, poetry, genealogy (five books: Genesis, Exodus, Leviticus, Numbers, Deuteronomy) · Israelite / Jewish canonical scripture
works.tagline: "In the beginning God created" — the foundational text of ethical monotheism, covenantal law, and the narrative of liberation

works.title: I Ching (Book of Changes, attributed arrangement)
works.authorDate: King Wen of Zhou (traditional attribution) · c. 1050–800 BCE (core hexagram and judgment layers; commentaries later)
works.form: Divination manual and cosmological treatise (64 hexagrams with judgments and line statements) · Chinese classical canon (one of the Five Classics)
works.tagline: "One yin and one yang — this is the Tao" — the oldest Chinese classic, encoding cosmic process in sixty-four binary figures

works.title: Instructions of Amenemope
works.form: Wisdom instruction (sebayt) in thirty chapters · Egyptian wisdom literature (sebayt)
works.tagline: "Better is bread when the heart is happy" — the quiet man as moral ideal, ma'at as cosmic order, and the oldest cross-cultural wisdom tradition

works.title: Book of Psalms (traditionally attributed)
works.authorDate: King David (traditional attribution; composite authorship per scholarly consensus) · c. 1000–300 BCE (traditional: c. 1000 BCE; critical: composed over centuries)
works.form: Lyric poetry — hymns, laments, thanksgivings, royal psalms, wisdom psalms (150 psalms in 5 books) · Israelite / Jewish liturgical and wisdom literature
works.tagline: "The LORD is my shepherd" — the full range of human religious experience in verse: praise, anguish, trust, rage, penitence, and ecstasy

works.title: Epic of Gilgamesh
works.authorDate: Anonymous / composite (Sin-leqi-unninni, c. 1200 BCE, final redactor) · c. 2100–1200 BCE (composite)
works.form: Epic poetry on clay tablets · Mesopotamian literary / religious tradition
works.tagline: He who saw the deep — the first epic, the first confrontation with mortality in world literature

works.title: Elijah Cycle (1 Kings 17 – 2 Kings 2)
works.authorDate: Anonymous (Deuteronomistic historians) · c. 9th–7th century BCE (events c. 870–850 BCE; written form c. 7th century)
works.form: Prophetic narrative embedded in historical books · Israelite prophetic / Deuteronomistic
works.tagline: The LORD, he is God — Elijah's fiery defence of monotheism and the quiet encounter on Horeb

works.title: Speeches in the Book of Documents (Shangshu)
works.authorDate: Duke of Zhou (attributed) · c. 1042 BCE (events); written form c. 10th–5th century BCE
works.form: Political speeches and proclamations · Chinese classical / Confucian canonical
works.tagline: Heaven's mandate is not constant — the founding speeches of Chinese political philosophy

works.title: Instructions for King Merikare
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to a Heracleopolitan pharaoh) · c. 2050 BCE
works.form: Wisdom literature / royal instruction · Egyptian wisdom tradition
works.tagline: Do justice that you may endure upon earth — the oldest political philosophy, grounded in ma'at

works.title: Dialogues in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to Yajnavalkya) · c. 8th–7th century BCE
works.form: Philosophical dialogues within a prose Upanishad · Vedic / proto-Vedanta
works.tagline: Neti neti — the dialogues that established the identity of Self and Absolute in Indian philosophy

works.title: Mahabharata (attributed)
works.authorDate: Vyasa (traditional attribution) · c. 400 BCE – 400 CE (composite; core narrative possibly older)
works.form: Epic poetry (approximately 200,000 verses in 18 books) · Hindu epic / Vedic-Brahmanical
works.tagline: What is here is found elsewhere; what is not here is nowhere — the encyclopedic epic of dharma

works.title: Papyrus of Ani (Book of the Dead)
works.authorDate: Scribal tradition (prepared for the scribe Ani) · c. 1250 BCE
works.form: Illustrated funerary papyrus scroll (78+ feet) · Egyptian funerary / religious
works.tagline: My heart, do not stand against me — the most beautiful surviving guide to the Egyptian afterlife

works.title: Library of Nineveh (curated collection)
works.authorDate: Ashurbanipal (patron and collector) · c. 668–631 BCE (collected; texts range from c. 2000 BCE onward)
works.form: Cuneiform clay tablet library (30,000+ tablets and fragments) · Mesopotamian scholarly / royal
works.tagline: For the sake of distant days — the first great library, preserving Mesopotamian civilisation on clay

works.title: Self-Praise Hymns of Shulgi
works.authorDate: Shulgi of Ur (attributed; court scribes) · c. 2094–2047 BCE
works.form: Royal hymns (Shulgi A, B, C, D, E, and others) · Sumerian royal literary tradition
works.tagline: I am a king, the weapon of the gods — the first celebration of royal excellence in athletics, learning, and the scribal arts

works.title: Sargon Birth Legend and Chronicle
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to Sargon in first person) · c. 2300–700 BCE (events c. 2334–2279 BCE; surviving copies c. 7th century)
works.form: Royal legend and chronicle on clay tablets · Mesopotamian royal literary tradition
works.tagline: My mother was a high priestess; my father I knew not — the first autobiography of a world-conqueror, the archetype of the self-made king

works.title: Gudea Cylinders
works.authorDate: Gudea of Lagash (attributed; court scribes) · c. 2144–2124 BCE
works.form: Temple-building hymn inscribed on clay cylinders · Sumerian religious / literary tradition
works.tagline: The house planted by the side of a river — the most elaborate surviving account of divine vision, sacred architecture, and temple dedication in Sumerian literature

works.title: Instructions of Kagemni
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to a sage addressing Kagemni) · c. 2300 BCE (original composition); surviving copy c. 1850 BCE (Papyrus Prisse)
works.form: Wisdom instruction (sebayt) · Egyptian wisdom literature
works.tagline: The quiet man prospers — one of the oldest surviving moral teachings, counselling restraint, modesty, and self-control

works.title: Song of Deborah (Judges 5)
works.authorDate: Deborah (attributed); anonymous (composite tradition) · c. 12th–11th century BCE
works.form: Victory hymn / poem · Israelite prophetic / poetic tradition
works.tagline: Awake, awake, Deborah! — one of the oldest Hebrew poems, celebrating female leadership, divine intervention, and the human cost of war

works.title: Book of Job
works.form: Wisdom dialogue (prose frame with extended poetic core) · Israelite / Jewish wisdom tradition
works.tagline: Where were you when I laid the foundation of the earth? — the most radical questioning of divine justice in ancient literature

works.title: Dialogue with Yajnavalkya (Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 3.6, 3.8)
works.authorDate: Anonymous (Upanishadic tradition); Gargi Vachaknavi as interlocutor · c. 7th century BCE
works.form: Philosophical dialogue embedded in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad · Vedic / early Upanishadic
works.tagline: On what is the whole world woven, warp and woof? — the relentless questioning that drove Upanishadic inquiry to its absolute limit

works.title: Apology of Hattusili III
works.form: Royal autobiography / political apology · Hittite royal literary tradition
works.tagline: Ishtar, my lady, always rescued me — the first autobiography, a usurper's masterwork of political self-justification through divine destiny

works.title: Dialogue on Immortality (Brihadaranyaka Upanishad 2.4, 4.5)
works.authorDate: Anonymous (attributed to Maitreyi and Yajnavalkya) · c. 7th century BCE
works.form: Philosophical dialogue embedded in the Brihadaranyaka Upanishad · Vedic / proto-Vedanta
works.tagline: "What should I do with that by which I do not become immortal?" — the question that made knowledge of the Self the supreme pursuit

works.title: Rig Vedic Hymns (Mandala 1, selected hymns attributed to Agastya)
works.form: Liturgical hymns (suktas) in metrical verse · Vedic / Rig Vedic
works.tagline: Sacred speech that yokes cosmic order to ritual fire — the seer's hymns to the storm-gods, the divine fire, and the king of the gods

works.title: Speeches in the Shangshu (Call of Shao and others)
works.authorDate: Duke of Shao (attributed) · c. 11th century BCE (events); written form c. 10th–5th century BCE
works.form: Political speeches preserved in the Shangshu (Book of Documents) · Zhou dynasty / early Chinese political thought
works.tagline: "The people are the root" — the speeches that grounded political legitimacy in popular welfare and the lessons of dynastic failure

works.title: Enmerkar and the Lord of Aratta
works.form: Epic/narrative poetry on clay tablets · Sumerian literary tradition
works.tagline: The messenger's mouth was too heavy — so the king invented the clay tablet: the origin myth of writing as the technology that overcomes the limits of memory and distance

works.title: Book of Ruth
works.authorDate: Anonymous · c. 6th–4th century BCE (literary form); set c. 1100 BCE
works.form: Short narrative (novella) in four chapters · Israelite / Hebrew Bible (Ketuvim / Writings)
works.tagline: "Where you go I will go" — the story of loyal love that made a Moabite woman the ancestor of David and challenged every boundary of belonging

works.title: Boundary Stelae and Amarna Inscriptions (attributed)
works.form: Monumental inscriptions (boundary stelae, temple reliefs, royal decrees) · Ancient Egyptian / Amarna theology
works.tagline: The sole god whose light creates all things — the inscriptions that defined the most radical theological revolution in ancient Egypt

works.title: Speeches and Narratives (1 Samuel 1-16)
works.authorDate: Anonymous (Deuteronomistic historians) · c. 7th–6th century BCE (written form); events c. 11th century BCE
works.form: Prophetic narrative and political speeches embedded in historical books · Israelite prophetic / Deuteronomistic
works.tagline: "This will be the manner of the king" — the narrative of the prophet who anointed kings while warning Israel that monarchy would cost them their freedom

personas.name: Winston Churchill
personas.description: Realism in the service of liberty, leavened by Anglican Providence and Stoic endurance

personas.name: Benjamin Franklin
personas.description: Deist Creator, empirical method, pragmatic morals — the Enlightenment in one tradesman's body

personas.name: Richard M. Nixon
personas.description: Quaker pieties on the surface, realpolitik underneath — the tension is the man

personas.name: Abraham Lincoln
personas.description: Reformed-tinged fatalism, hard prairie realism, Stoic endurance under impossible weight

personas.name: Thomas Jefferson
personas.description: Enlightenment Deism, Lockean empiricism, naturalist confidence — the moral of Jesus without the metaphysics

personas.name: Ralph Waldo Emerson
personas.description: One Over-Soul behind many faces; Nature as the visible spirit; the self as a channel of the divine

personas.name: Martin Luther King Jr.
personas.description: Personalist theism plus Gandhian non-violence plus a Reformed sense that the moral universe bends

personas.name: Marcus Aurelius
personas.description: A working emperor's Stoicism: cosmic order, accepted fate, daily duty, no consolation but the next right act

personas.name: C. S. Lewis
personas.description: Mere Christianity defended by a Platonist-Realist who loved the medieval cosmos

personas.name: Bertrand Russell
personas.description: Analytic logic, empirical method, naturalist cosmos, dignified atheism

personas.name: Augustine of Hippo
personas.description: Christian Platonism with a Pauline backbone — predestination, original sin, and the eternal Now of God

personas.name: Thomas Aquinas
personas.description: The Aristotelian-Christian synthesis: reason in service of revelation, nature as God's grammar

personas.name: Mohandas K. Gandhi
personas.description: Advaita non-duality plus Jain ahimsa plus the Sermon on the Mount — truth as the highest god

personas.name: Friedrich Nietzsche
personas.description: Naturalism without consolation, nihilism diagnosed, the will to power as the deepest fact

personas.name: Simone Weil
personas.description: Christian Platonism on the factory floor — affliction as the only honest theology

personas.name: Confucius (Kongzi)
personas.description: Heaven's mandate, ritual propriety, and the cultivation of humaneness through patient practice

personas.name: Hannah Arendt
personas.description: The vita activa — speech, action, natality — as the only reply to totalitarianism

personas.name: Albert Einstein
personas.description: Block-universe realism plus Spinozist reverence — physics is religious in its objectivity

personas.name: Frederick Douglass
personas.description: Bedrock realism about slavery and power, Christian prophecy turned against pseudo-Christian masters

personas.name: Dietrich Bonhoeffer
personas.description: Costly grace, religionless Christianity, and the discipline of "this-worldly" discipleship

personas.name: Lyndon B. Johnson
personas.description: Disciples-of-Christ practical Christianity in service of New Deal expansionist realism

personas.name: Gerald R. Ford
personas.description: Episcopalian Midwestern moderation — institutions and decency above ideology

personas.name: James Earl Carter Jr.
personas.description: Southern Baptist conscience plus human-rights universalism plus engineering pragmatism

personas.name: Ronald W. Reagan
personas.description: Anti-communist providentialism, Hollywood-mythic American exceptionalism, optimistic Disciples piety

personas.name: George H. W. Bush
personas.description: Episcopalian Connecticut-Texan establishment realism — duty, prudence, and a thousand handwritten notes

personas.name: William J. Clinton
personas.description: Southern Baptist Third Way — pragmatist triangulation in service of an optimistic globalist liberalism

personas.name: George W. Bush
personas.description: Born-again Methodist evangelical certainty, post-9/11 democratic-realist ambition

personas.name: Barack H. Obama
personas.description: Niebuhrian Christian realism, pragmatist deliberation, cosmopolitan liberal universalism

personas.name: Donald J. Trump
personas.description: Norman-Vincent-Peale positivity, transactional realism, deal-making as the deepest virtue

personas.name: Joseph R. Biden Jr.
personas.description: Irish-Catholic Mass-attending Personalism, Senate-floor institutional realism, working-class New Deal liberalism

personas.name: Socrates
personas.description: The examined life, the daimonic sign, the death-as-argument: dialectic as the only honest path to virtue

personas.name: Plato
personas.description: The Forms are more real than what we see; the visible world is a shadow whose original is intelligible only to the philosophical soul

personas.name: Aristotle
personas.description: Hylomorphism, the four causes, eudaimonia through virtue — the working metaphysics of two thousand years of Western science and theology

personas.name: Laozi (Lao Tzu)
personas.description: The Way that can be spoken is not the eternal Way — wu-wei, the watercourse, the soft overcoming the hard

personas.name: Siddhārtha Gautama (the Buddha)
personas.description: Four noble truths, eightfold path, dependent origination — suffering analysed and the way out described

personas.name: Epicurus
personas.description: Atomism without fear, friendship as the highest external good, pleasure as the absence of pain

personas.name: Heraclitus of Ephesus
personas.description: Everything flows — fire as the cosmic principle, the unity of opposites, the river that is never twice the same

personas.name: Marcus Tullius Cicero
personas.description: Roman public Stoicism with Academic-Sceptic reservations — natural law as the working philosophy of the Republic

personas.name: Lucius Annaeus Seneca
personas.description: Stoic ethics for working Romans — the Letters to Lucilius as the most practical philosophical handbook of antiquity

personas.name: Paul of Tarsus (Saul / Saint Paul)
personas.description: Justification by grace through faith, the body as temple, the new creation in Christ — Pharisaic Judaism reread through resurrection

personas.name: Ibn Sīnā (Avicenna)
personas.description: The distinction between essence and existence, the Necessary Being, the floating-man argument — Aristotelian metaphysics in Islamic dress

personas.name: Moses Maimonides (Rambam)
personas.description: Aristotle in service of Torah — apophatic theology, philosophical allegoresis, the Thirteen Principles

personas.name: Ibn Rushd (Averroes)
personas.description: Defender of philosophy against al-Ghazālī, commentator par excellence on Aristotle, advocate of the unity of the intellect

personas.name: Jalāl al-Dīn Muḥammad Rūmī
personas.description: The Beloved as the only reality, the reed cut from the reed-bed crying to return — love as the deepest knowledge of God

personas.name: Hildegard of Bingen
personas.description: Viriditas — the greening power of God in all things; nature as a living theophany

personas.name: Dante Alighieri
personas.description: The Comedy as a complete moral cosmology — Thomistic theology, classical poetry, and Tuscan vernacular fused

personas.name: Meister Eckhart (Eckhart von Hochheim)
personas.description: The Godhead beyond God, the spark of the soul, detachment as the path to the divine ground

personas.name: Julian of Norwich
personas.description: All shall be well — divine love as the substance of reality, despite sin and suffering

personas.name: Martin Luther
personas.description: Justification by faith alone, the bound will, scripture as the church's sole final authority

personas.name: John Calvin (Jean Cauvin)
personas.description: The systematic Reformer — the sovereignty of God, predestination, the threefold use of the law

personas.name: René Descartes
personas.description: Cogito ergo sum — the thinking self as the indubitable starting point, the mind-body distinction as the metaphysical pivot

personas.name: Baruch (Benedict) Spinoza
personas.description: Deus sive Natura — one substance with infinite attributes; the geometric demonstration of God, mind, and freedom

personas.name: John Locke
personas.description: The mind as tabula rasa, government by consent, religious toleration — the scaffolding of the modern liberal order

personas.name: Blaise Pascal
personas.description: The heart has its reasons that reason knows not — Jansenist Augustinianism, the wager, the misery and greatness of man

personas.name: Galileo Galilei
personas.description: Mathematics as the language of nature, experimental method as its grammar — and a Catholicism that meant the trial of 1633

personas.name: William Shakespeare
personas.description: No systematic philosophy — but a working metaphysics so capacious every later age has read its own questions back into the plays

personas.name: Immanuel Kant
personas.description: Space, time, and the categories as the mind's contribution to experience; the categorical imperative; the starry heavens above and the moral law within

personas.name: David Hume
personas.description: Custom is the great guide of life — induction has no rational ground, the self is a bundle of perceptions, miracles are not to be believed

personas.name: Adam Smith
personas.description: The impartial spectator, sympathy as the foundation of morals, the invisible hand of the market — and the limits of all three

personas.name: Voltaire (François-Marie Arouet)
personas.description: Écrasez l'infâme — crush the infamous thing; Deism plus empirical method plus relentless wit

personas.name: Jean-Jacques Rousseau
personas.description: Man is born free, and everywhere he is in chains — the general will, the noble savage, the autobiographical self

personas.name: Georg Wilhelm Friedrich Hegel
personas.description: The real is the rational; Spirit unfolds dialectically through history toward absolute self-knowledge

personas.name: Mary Wollstonecraft
personas.description: Reason as the human birthright; if it belongs to men it belongs to women — the rights of woman are the rights of man

personas.name: Søren Kierkegaard
personas.description: Subjectivity is truth — the leap of faith, the knight of resignation, the offence of the God-man

personas.name: Karl Marx
personas.description: The material conditions of production are the substrate of history; philosophy has interpreted the world, the point is to change it

personas.name: Charles Darwin
personas.description: Descent with modification by natural selection — the single biological mechanism that reorganised the life sciences and challenged most of Western theology

personas.name: Henry David Thoreau
personas.description: Simplify, simplify — Walden Pond as the laboratory of the examined life; the night in jail as the manifesto of civil disobedience

personas.name: Lev Nikolayevich Tolstoy
personas.description: The Kingdom of God is within you — Christian anarchism, nonviolent resistance, the rejection of the State and the Church alike

personas.name: Fyodor Mikhailovich Dostoevsky
personas.description: If God does not exist, everything is permitted — the Grand Inquisitor, the Idiot, the Karamazov brothers as theological case studies

personas.name: Jean-Paul Sartre
personas.description: Existence precedes essence — radical freedom, bad faith, the project of self-creation against the indifference of being

personas.name: Simone de Beauvoir
personas.description: One is not born, but rather becomes, a woman — existentialist freedom met by the structural situation of women

personas.name: Albert Camus
personas.description: The myth of Sisyphus — we must imagine him happy; revolt against the absurd as the basis of an ethics without metaphysical foundation

personas.name: Carl Gustav Jung
personas.description: The collective unconscious, the archetypes, individuation as the lifelong task — psychology re-opening doors that nineteenth-century materialism had closed

personas.name: Sigmund Freud
personas.description: The unconscious is the seat of repression; civilization is built on its discontents; religion is a universal obsessional neurosis

personas.name: Virginia Woolf
personas.description: Moments of being against the cotton-wool of daily life — phenomenology of consciousness in novelistic form

personas.name: Thomas Stearns Eliot
personas.description: In my beginning is my end — modernist poetic technique married to Anglo-Catholic theology in the Four Quartets

personas.name: Thomas Merton
personas.description: Cistercian contemplation opened onto Zen, Sufism, and the social conscience of the 1960s — Catholic mysticism with a Buddhist accent

personas.name: Václav Havel
personas.description: Living in truth — the power of the powerless, the politics of conscience under a regime of lies

personas.name: Nelson Rolihlahla Mandela
personas.description: Ubuntu — I am because we are; twenty-seven years in prison answered with reconciliation

personas.name: Karol Józef Wojtyła / Pope John Paul II
personas.description: Phenomenology of the person plus Thomistic metaphysics — the Catholic Church's most consequential modern intellectual papacy

personas.name: Plotinus
personas.description: From the One emanates Nous, from Nous Soul, from Soul matter — and the philosophical life is the soul's patient return

personas.name: Edmund Husserl
personas.description: Back to the things themselves — the epoché, intentionality, and the rigorous description of consciousness as it actually appears

personas.name: Friedrich Schleiermacher
personas.description: Religion as the feeling of absolute dependence — the founding figure of liberal Protestant theology

personas.name: Gustavo Gutiérrez
personas.description: The preferential option for the poor — theology as the second act, after the first act of standing alongside the oppressed

personas.name: William Franklin "Billy" Graham
personas.description: The simple gospel preached on six continents — the most public face of twentieth-century evangelical Protestantism

personas.name: Tenzin Gyatso, 14th Dalai Lama
personas.description: Compassion as the universal religion, emptiness as the philosophical substrate, dialogue with science as the modern test

personas.name: Joseph Smith Jr.
personas.description: A restoration in upstate New York — pre-mortal souls, eternal matter, exalted human destiny

personas.name: Guru Nānak Dev Ji
personas.description: Ik Onkar — One God, no Hindu, no Muslim; devotion (bhakti), honest work, and sharing as the threefold path

personas.name: Zarathustra (Zoroaster)
personas.description: Ahura Mazda against Angra Mainyu — the first dualistic cosmology, the cosmic battle of light against darkness, the moral choice as cosmic act

personas.name: Shinran
personas.description: Other-power (tariki) over self-power — salvation entirely by Amida Buddha's vow, the nembutsu as gratitude rather than merit

personas.name: Wole Soyinka
personas.description: Ogun as the deity of the tragic boundary — Yoruba metaphysics in dialogue with Greek tragedy and global modernism

personas.name: Octavia E. Butler
personas.description: God is change — Earthseed as evolutionary theology, the patient anatomy of survival under conditions designed for failure

personas.name: Aldous Huxley
personas.description: The doors of perception — the perennial philosophy, mescaline-mediated mysticism, the unitive ground beneath the world's religions

personas.name: Arne Næss
personas.description: Self-realisation through the wider ecological Self — the equal intrinsic worth of all living beings

personas.name: Audre Lorde
personas.description: "There is no hierarchy of oppressions" — the uses of the erotic, the master's tools, the warrior-poet's discipline of voice

personas.name: Alfred North Whitehead
personas.description: Becoming over being — actual occasions as the atomic units of reality, God as fellow sufferer who understands

personas.name: Ludwig Wittgenstein
personas.description: The limits of my language are the limits of my world — two great philosophical projects, each repudiating the other

personas.name: Claude Lévi-Strauss
personas.description: Myths think themselves in human minds — the universal grammar of binary opposition behind every cultural particular

personas.name: Michel Foucault
personas.description: Power produces knowledge; knowledge produces subjects — discipline, the panopticon, the genealogy of the modern self

personas.name: Abū Ḥāmid al-Ghazālī
personas.description: Reason within its limits, mystical certainty beyond them — the Ash'arite synthesis of philosophy, law, and Sufism

personas.name: Bahá'u'lláh (Mírzá Ḥusayn-ʻAlí Núrí)
personas.description: The progressive revelation of one God across the world's religious traditions — humanity entering its age of maturity

personas.name: Daisetsu Teitarō Suzuki
personas.description: Satori as the breakthrough beyond conceptualisation — Zen as the perennial possibility within Mahayana Buddhism

personas.name: Yuval Noah Harari
personas.description: Dataism as the emerging religion of the data age — humans as biological algorithms, history as the cumulative storytelling of fictions

personas.name: Madhvācārya
personas.description: Five eternal distinctions — God, souls, and matter are genuinely different; the most uncompromising theistic Vedanta

personas.name: Roy Bhaskar
personas.description: The real, the actual, the empirical — a stratified ontology in which science discovers generative mechanisms

personas.name: Richard Rorty
personas.description: Truth is what your peers will let you get away with saying — solidarity over objectivity, conversation over correspondence

personas.name: Pierre-Simon Laplace
personas.description: Laplace's demon — given the position and momentum of every particle, the future is fixed

personas.name: Arthur Norman Prior
personas.description: Only the present is real — tense as logical primitive, the open future as ontological fact

personas.name: David Deutsch
personas.description: Other universes are not metaphor — quantum mechanics, taken literally, requires the multiverse

personas.name: Nick Bostrom
personas.description: The simulation argument and existential risk — taking the long-run future of intelligent life as a philosophical subject

personas.name: John Stuart Mill
personas.description: Permanent possibilities of sensation — phenomenalism, the harm principle, women's suffrage as natural consequences

personas.name: Graham Harman
personas.description: Objects withdraw from their relations — flat ontology, the end of correlationism

personas.name: Isaac ben Solomon Luria (the Ari)
personas.description: Tzimtzum, shevirat ha-kelim, tikkun — God's self-contraction, the breaking of the vessels, the cosmic repair as the human task

personas.name: William James
personas.description: Pure experience as the neutral fabric — radical empiricism, pragmatic truth, the varieties of religious experience

personas.name: David J. Chalmers
personas.description: The hard problem of consciousness — and virtual reality is genuine reality

personas.name: Motoori Norinaga
personas.description: Mono no aware — Japanese sensibility against Chinese-Confucian intellectualisation; Shinto as substrate

personas.name: Parmenides of Elea
personas.description: What is, is; what is not, is not — the path of Truth (Aletheia) against the path of Opinion (Doxa)

personas.name: Hypatia of Alexandria
personas.description: Reason as the only path to the Good — late-antique Platonism teaching mathematics, astronomy, and philosophy in a Christianizing Alexandria

personas.name: Diogenes of Sinope (the Cynic)
personas.description: Live according to nature against convention — virtue through ascetic shamelessness

personas.name: Anicius Manlius Severinus Boethius
personas.description: The Consolation of Philosophy from a prison cell — Fortune, the Good, and divine eternity as "the simultaneously whole and perfect possession of interminable life"

personas.name: Quintus Septimius Florens Tertullianus
personas.description: "What has Athens to do with Jerusalem?" — early Latin Christianity against pagan philosophy, then against Catholic compromise

personas.name: Anselm of Canterbury
personas.description: Faith seeking understanding — the ontological argument, satisfaction theory of atonement, faith and reason as one project

personas.name: William of Ockham
personas.description: Entities should not be multiplied beyond necessity — radical nominalism, divine voluntarism, the via moderna

personas.name: Catherine of Siena (Caterina Benincasa)
personas.description: Mystical marriage to Christ, political letters that brought a pope back to Rome — late-medieval Italian Catholicism at full intensity

personas.name: Nicholas of Cusa (Nicolaus Cusanus)
personas.description: Learned ignorance and the coincidence of opposites — God as the infinite circle whose centre is everywhere

personas.name: John Scotus Eriugena (Iohannes Scottus Eriugena)
personas.description: Neoplatonism in Latin dress — the four divisions of nature, the eventual return of all things to God

personas.name: Desiderius Erasmus of Rotterdam
personas.description: Christian humanism — the philosophia Christi recovered from Scripture and the Church Fathers against medieval scholastic complication

personas.name: Thomas Hobbes
personas.description: The Leviathan — the state of nature as war of all against all, sovereignty as the only escape

personas.name: Sir Isaac Newton
personas.description: Absolute space, absolute time, the law of universal gravitation — and millions of words on biblical prophecy and alchemy

personas.name: Gottfried Wilhelm Leibniz
personas.description: The best of all possible worlds — monads, pre-established harmony, the principle of sufficient reason

personas.name: George Berkeley
personas.description: Esse est percipi — to be is to be perceived. Material substance is incoherent; only minds and ideas exist.

personas.name: Jonathan Edwards
personas.description: The greatest American Reformed theologian — Calvinist sovereignty, religious affections, and a metaphysics in which beings depend continuously on divine perception

personas.name: John Wesley
personas.description: Christian perfection through prevenient grace, free will, and the means of grace — the world is my parish

personas.name: Arthur Schopenhauer
personas.description: The world as will and representation — the Will as the noumenon, life as suffering, denial of the will as the only way out

personas.name: Johann Wolfgang von Goethe
personas.description: The Spinozist polymath at the heart of German classicism — Faust, the Theory of Colors, the Urpflanze

personas.name: Henri Bergson
personas.description: Durée — time as lived qualitative duration, against the scientific spatialization of time; the élan vital as the creative principle of evolution

personas.name: Martin Heidegger
personas.description: Sein und Zeit — the question of Being recovered through the existential analysis of Dasein

personas.name: Martin Buber
personas.description: I and Thou — the irreducibility of dialogical relation as the ground of personal and divine reality

personas.name: Emmanuel Levinas
personas.description: Ethics as first philosophy — the face of the Other commands me before any ontology

personas.name: Walter Benjamin
personas.description: The angel of history blown backwards by the storm of progress; messianic time interrupting historicist time

personas.name: Jürgen Habermas
personas.description: Communicative reason — validity claims redeemable through ideal-speech dialogue as the post-metaphysical ground of legitimacy

personas.name: Karl Barth
personas.description: Nein! to natural theology — revelation is God's self-disclosure, against all human religious projection

personas.name: Karl Rahner
personas.description: The supernatural existential — every human being is oriented to God in the depths of their being, whether they know it or not

personas.name: Reinhold Niebuhr
personas.description: Christian realism — the persistent reality of sin in collective life constrains every utopian political project

personas.name: James Cone
personas.description: God is Black — theology done from the underside of American racial history

personas.name: Howard Thurman
personas.description: "Jesus and the Disinherited" — Christian mysticism as the inner resource of nonviolent resistance

personas.name: Iris Murdoch
personas.description: Attention to the real — moral life is the loving, just attention to what is actually there, against the ego's consoling fantasies

personas.name: Alasdair MacIntyre
personas.description: "After Virtue" — modern moral discourse is the wreckage of an Aristotelian tradition we no longer have

personas.name: Cornel West
personas.description: Prophetic pragmatism — radical democratic Christianity drawing on Du Bois, James, Dewey, and the Black Baptist church

personas.name: bell hooks
personas.description: Love as the practice of freedom — feminist theory rooted in the lived intersection of race, gender, and class

personas.name: Mary Daly
personas.description: Be-ing the verb — radical-lesbian feminism as a metaphysical leap out of patriarchal religion

personas.name: Stephen Hawking
personas.description: Black-hole thermodynamics, the no-boundary proposal, and a popularized atheism of "the universe needs no creator"

personas.name: Richard Dawkins
personas.description: The Selfish Gene — replicator-centric evolutionary biology with a militant naturalist polemics against religion

personas.name: Daniel Kahneman
personas.description: Thinking, Fast and Slow — the dual-process architecture of human reasoning and its systematic biases

personas.name: Carl Sagan
personas.description: "We are made of star-stuff" — scientific naturalism as a reverent humanism of the cosmos

personas.name: Donna Haraway
personas.description: Cyborg manifesto and companion species — feminist science studies of human-machine-animal entanglement

personas.name: Rabindranath Tagore
personas.description: Universal humanism in a Vedantic-Brahmo register — the divine encountered in the human and the natural

personas.name: Muhammad Iqbal
personas.description: Khudi (selfhood) — Islamic reconstruction of religious thought in dialogue with Bergson, Nietzsche, and Whitehead

personas.name: Lu Xun
personas.description: "A Madman's Diary" and "The True Story of Ah Q" — fierce diagnoses of Chinese cultural disease, written in baihua vernacular

personas.name: Yukio Mishima
personas.description: Aesthetic ultranationalism — the body, the sword, and the Emperor as recovered Japanese essence against post-war pacifist materialism

personas.name: Pope Francis (Jorge Mario Bergoglio)
personas.description: "Laudato Si'" and "Fratelli Tutti" — Catholic social teaching of integral ecology and human fraternity

personas.name: Toni Morrison
personas.description: "Beloved" — the ghostly recovered presence of slavery's unspeakable past; the unsaid as the heart of African American memory

personas.name: James Baldwin
personas.description: "The Fire Next Time" — prophetic-essayistic Christianity diagnosing American racial guilt

personas.name: Dorothy Day
personas.description: Catholic Worker — voluntary poverty, hospitality houses, and Christian-pacifist resistance to war and capital

personas.name: Frantz Fanon
personas.description: "The Wretched of the Earth" — psychiatric and political analysis of colonial violence and revolutionary humanism

personas.name: Eleanor Roosevelt
personas.description: Human-rights universalism — pragmatic-Episcopalian liberalism translated into the foundational document of post-war international ethics

personas.name: Pythagoras of Samos
personas.description: All is number — the cosmos as harmonic-mathematical order, ritually disclosed to the initiated community

personas.name: Mani
personas.description: Cosmic dualism — the eternal war of Light and Darkness, with redemption through gnosis and ascetic separation

personas.name: Īśvarakṛṣṇa
personas.description: Sāṃkhyakārikā — the seventy verses establishing the dualism of puruṣa (consciousness) and prakṛti (primordial nature)

personas.name: Helena Petrovna Blavatsky
personas.description: "The Secret Doctrine" — Theosophy as the universal "perennial wisdom" behind all religions, with astral and subtle-energy layers

personas.name: Heȟáka Sápa (Black Elk)
personas.description: The hoop of the people — Lakota spirit-relational metaphysics as the world is given, the sacred pipe at its center

personas.name: Thomas Kuhn
personas.description: Paradigm shifts — scientific knowledge as a tradition-constituted social practice punctuated by revolutionary reorganizations

personas.name: David Bohm
personas.description: The implicate order — quantum mechanics as the surface of a hidden non-local wholeness

personas.name: Hilary Putnam
personas.description: Truth, realism, and the rejection of metaphysical realism — the pragmatic realist who repeatedly rebuilt his position when honesty required it

personas.name: Ernst Mach
personas.description: Sensations as the elements — radical empiricist phenomenalism that shaped Einstein, Vienna Circle, and the relational tradition

personas.name: Rudolf Carnap
personas.description: Logical syntax of language — the elimination of metaphysics through the formal analysis of scientific discourse

personas.name: Hugh Everett III
personas.description: The universal wavefunction — all measurement outcomes are realized in branching parallel worlds

personas.name: Vasubandhu
personas.description: Vijñaptimātratā — "mere cognition" — the world as the structured unfolding of consciousness

personas.name: Nicolas Malebranche
personas.description: "We see all things in God" — occasionalism and the vision-in-God as the radical Cartesianism that solves the mind-body problem

personas.name: Protagoras of Abdera
personas.description: "Man is the measure of all things" — the homo-mensura doctrine as the first explicit philosophical relativism

personas.name: Zhuangzi (Zhuang Zhou)
personas.description: The butterfly dream and the carefree wandering — Daoist skepticism and the relativization of every fixed perspective

personas.name: John Archibald Wheeler
personas.description: "It from bit" — information as the ultimate physical substrate, prior to matter and energy

personas.name: Desmond Tutu
personas.description: Ubuntu and prophetic Christianity — "I am because we are," translated into the moral architecture of post-apartheid reconciliation

personas.name: Tsongkhapa Losang Drakpa
personas.description: Madhyamaka–Prāsaṅgika rigor — the precise philosophical formulation of emptiness, with monastic-scholastic training as the bedrock of awakening

personas.name: C. D. Broad
personas.description: The specious present and the growing-block universe — careful philosophical analysis between A-theory and B-theory

personas.name: J. M. E. McTaggart
personas.description: The unreality of time — the A-series / B-series distinction that founded analytic philosophy of time

personas.name: Bruno Latour
personas.description: Actor-network theory — non-humans as actors; "we have never been modern"

personas.name: Jaron Lanier
personas.description: VR pioneer turned digital humanist — virtual realism with serious skepticism about platform-corporation extraction

personas.name: Terence McKenna
personas.description: Logos through the mushroom — psychedelics as the engines of human cultural evolution and the bridge to the transcendent other

personas.name: Sun Ra (Herman Poole Blount)
personas.description: Space is the place — Black liberation as cosmic departure into the future on the wings of the Arkestra

personas.name: Brigham Young
personas.description: The Lion of the Lord — institutional builder who carried Joseph Smith's revelations into the kingdom-in-the-wilderness

personas.name: Guru Gobind Singh
personas.description: Saint-soldier — the Khalsa as the embodied community of disciplined sovereignty under divine command

personas.name: Raghavendra Swami
personas.description: Madhva's heir — rigorous philosophical commentary on Dvaita theism and the saint-tradition of bhakti devotion

personas.name: Gottlob Frege
personas.description: Begriffsschrift — the logical-language reform that founded analytic philosophy

personas.name: Kurt Gödel
personas.description: The incompleteness theorems and ontological Platonism — mathematical truth that outruns formal proof

personas.name: Alan Turing
personas.description: The Turing machine and the imitation game — computation as the foundation of mind and the substrate of information

personas.name: Saul Kripke
personas.description: Rigid designators and the necessity of identity — modal metaphysics reconstructed against the descriptivist tradition

personas.name: Derek Parfit
personas.description: Reasons and Persons — personal identity is not what matters; ethics is what we have most reason to do

personas.name: David Lewis
personas.description: Modal realism — all possible worlds are real worlds, no less real than the actual one

personas.name: Nāgārjuna
personas.description: All dharmas are empty — the dialectical reductio of every position whatsoever to dependent origination

personas.name: Wang Yangming
personas.description: Liangzhi (innate knowledge of the good) — the unity of knowledge and action against Zhu Xi's gradualism

personas.name: Mencius (Mengzi)
personas.description: The four sprouts — human nature is innately good; benevolent government cultivates what is naturally there

personas.name: Nishida Kitarō
personas.description: Pure experience and the logic of basho (place) — Zen Buddhist categories systematized in dialogue with Western philosophy

personas.name: Sri Aurobindo
personas.description: Integral yoga and supramental descent — modern Indian philosophy of evolutionary ascent toward divine consciousness

personas.name: Mulla Sadra (Sadr al-Din al-Shirazi)
personas.description: The transcendent theosophy (al-Hikmat al-Muta'aliya) — the primacy of existence (asalat al-wujud) and substantial motion

personas.name: Achille Mbembe
personas.description: Necropolitics — the contemporary political condition as sovereignty over death; African postcolonial critique against the conscious-Western imperial subject

personas.name: Vladimir Solovyov
personas.description: Godmanhood and Sophia — the cosmic-historical project of divine-human unity in Eastern Christian register

personas.name: Nikolai Berdyaev
personas.description: Freedom is the deepest reality — primordial uncreated freedom prior to being, the meaning of personality, the destiny of the creative act

personas.name: Vine Deloria Jr.
personas.description: Indigenous metaphysics against the Western evolutionary-historicist conceit — "we are the people, this is the place, this is the moment"

personas.name: Democritus of Abdera
personas.description: Atoms and the void — only atoms in the void are real; everything else is convention

personas.name: G. E. M. Anscombe (Elizabeth Anscombe)
personas.description: Intention and "Modern Moral Philosophy" — the recovery of virtue ethics and the action-theoretic foundations of moral psychology

personas.name: Edith Stein (Saint Teresa Benedicta of the Cross)
personas.description: Phenomenology of empathy and finite-and-eternal being — Husserlian method oriented toward Thomistic metaphysics

personas.name: Martha Nussbaum
personas.description: Capabilities, emotions, and the fragility of goodness — Aristotelian virtue ethics in dialogue with contemporary feminism and development economics

personas.name: Peter Singer
personas.description: Animal Liberation and effective altruism — preference utilitarianism applied with maximum consistency, comfort be damned

personas.name: Samuel Clarke
personas.description: Newton's philosophical voice — substantival space-time, divine voluntarism, and the rational defence of Christianity

personas.name: Niels Bohr
personas.description: Complementarity, the measurement cut, and the unfinished business of quantum reality

personas.name: Frederick Copleston
personas.description: The cleanest 20th-century Catholic-Thomistic engagement with analytic atheism

personas.name: Pelagius
personas.description: Human moral power retained after the Fall — and condemned for saying so

personas.name: John Bramhall
personas.description: Libertarian free will against Hobbesian necessity, defended in exile and from the episcopate

personas.name: Robert Hooke
personas.description: Microscopist, mechanical philosopher, and the Newtonian era's frustrated near-genius

personas.name: Willard Van Orman Quine
personas.description: No first philosophy: epistemology, ontology, and semantics naturalised into one continuous web

personas.name: Noam Chomsky
personas.description: Innate language faculty, principled naturalism, anarcho-syndicalist political vision

personas.name: Siger of Brabant
personas.description: Aristotelian philosophy followed wherever it leads — including against Christian doctrine

personas.name: Robert Bellarmine
personas.description: The intellectual conscience of the Counter-Reformation papacy

personas.name: Gaunilo of Marmoutiers
personas.description: "On behalf of the fool" — the first sustained refutation of the ontological argument

personas.name: Hans-Georg Gadamer
personas.description: Tradition, fusion of horizons, the rehabilitation of prejudice as a condition of understanding

personas.name: Richard Wagner
personas.description: Music-drama, the Gesamtkunstwerk, and a half-philosophical, half-mythical aesthetic of cultural renewal

personas.name: Xunzi
personas.description: Human nature is evil; goodness is the achievement of ritual and learning

personas.name: Adi Śaṅkara
personas.description: Brahman alone is real, the world is appearance, the self is Brahman

personas.name: Maṇḍana Miśra
personas.description: Ritual action and the reality of the world, with non-dualism arrived at by inference

personas.name: Ernst Cassirer
personas.description: Mathematics, science, language, myth, art, religion — each a symbolic form disclosing objective spirit

personas.name: Karl Popper
personas.description: Falsifiability, bold conjectures, and the open society

personas.name: Theodor Adorno
personas.description: Negative dialectic, the culture industry, and the totality of late-capitalist deformation

personas.name: John Searle
personas.description: Speech acts, the Chinese Room, biological naturalism, and the construction of social reality

personas.name: Jacques Derrida
personas.description: Différance, the absent centre, and the deconstruction of metaphysical oppositions

personas.name: William Whewell
personas.description: Colligation of facts, consilience of inductions, and the active mind in scientific discovery

personas.name: Edward Stillingfleet
personas.description: A latitudinarian defender of orthodoxy who pressed Locke hard on substance and the Trinity

personas.name: Mozi
personas.description: Impartial caring, anti-ritualism, and the first systematic consequentialist ethics in world philosophy

personas.name: Michael Servetus
personas.description: Anti-Trinitarian biblicism, pioneering pulmonary circulation, and burnt at the stake in Calvin's Geneva

personas.name: Robert Stalnaker
personas.description: Possible-worlds semantics for conditionals, propositions, and pragmatic context

personas.name: Thales of Miletus
personas.description: Water as arche — the first recorded attempt to explain the cosmos through a single natural principle

personas.name: Anaximander of Miletus
personas.description: The apeiron (boundless) as origin of all things, cosmic justice, and the first cosmological model

personas.name: Empedocles of Acragas
personas.description: Four roots (earth, air, fire, water) mixed and separated by Love and Strife in an eternal cosmic cycle

personas.name: Anaxagoras of Clazomenae
personas.description: Nous (Mind) as the ordering principle of a cosmos where everything contains a portion of everything

personas.name: Zeno of Elea
personas.description: The paradoxes of motion — Achilles and the tortoise, the flying arrow, the stadium — defending Parmenidean monism through reductio ad absurdum

personas.name: Zeno of Citium
personas.description: Founder of Stoicism — virtue as the only good, living according to nature, the rational cosmos pervaded by pneuma

personas.name: Chrysippus of Soli
personas.description: The second founder of Stoicism — Stoic logic (the five indemonstrables), compatibilist fate, cosmic conflagration, preferred indifferents

personas.name: Titus Lucretius Carus
personas.description: De Rerum Natura — the great Latin poem on atoms, void, mortal soul, indifferent gods, and the liberation of humanity from superstitious fear

personas.name: Epictetus
personas.description: We are disturbed not by things but by our judgments about things — and judgments are the one thing within our power

personas.name: Philo of Alexandria
personas.description: Moses spoke Greek before the Greeks — Torah read through a Platonic-Stoic lens, with Logos as the bridge

personas.name: Origen of Alexandria
personas.description: All souls pre-exist, all will be restored — the most daring systematic theology before Augustine

personas.name: Sextus Empiricus
personas.description: Suspend judgment on all doctrines and find tranquillity in the silence that follows

personas.name: Porphyry
personas.description: Logic as the gateway to Being — the Isagoge that shaped a millennium, the vegetarianism that shamed one

personas.name: Han Feizi
personas.description: Law, technique, and authority — the three handles by which a ruler governs without relying on virtue

personas.name: Patanjali
personas.description: Yoga is the cessation of the fluctuations of the mind — still the waves, and the seer rests in its own nature

personas.name: Aeschylus
personas.description: Divine justice working through suffering, the Furies transformed into civic law, the polis as moral order

personas.name: Sophocles
personas.description: Fate, the limits of human knowledge, the hero who sees too late — tragic wisdom as the price of self-knowledge

personas.name: Euripides
personas.description: The gods questioned, the passions unmasked — tragedy turned inward to the psychology of extremity

personas.name: Herodotus
personas.description: The customs of peoples, the reversals of fortune, divine envy of excess — history as inquiry into human and divine causation

personas.name: Thucydides
personas.description: Human nature as the constant, power as the driver, the Melian Dialogue as the anatomy of empire — history stripped of the gods

personas.name: Hippocrates of Cos
personas.description: Disease as natural process, not divine punishment — observation, prognosis, and the oath that bound medicine to ethics

personas.name: Xenophon
personas.description: Socrates as practical moralist, the march of the Ten Thousand, the art of command — philosophy as a guide to action

personas.name: Isocrates
personas.description: Rhetoric as the art of citizenship, paideia as the formation of the political soul, Panhellenism as a civilising ideal

personas.name: Polybius
personas.description: Universal history, the rise of Rome explained, anacyclosis as political science — the cycle of constitutions and the genius of the mixed regime

personas.name: Clement of Alexandria
personas.description: Faith seeking understanding through Greek philosophy — the Christian gnostic who baptised Plato

personas.name: Irenaeus of Lyon
personas.description: Against Heresies — the recapitulation of all things in Christ against gnostic dualism and the demiurge

personas.name: Athanasius of Alexandria
personas.description: Athanasius contra mundum — the Word became flesh so that we might become God

personas.name: Basil of Caesarea (Basil the Great)
personas.description: One ousia, three hypostaseis — Trinitarian theology, monastic rule, and the six days of creation read as divine pedagogy

personas.name: Gregory of Nyssa
personas.description: The infinite God beyond all knowing — epektasis, the soul's endless advance into the divine darkness

personas.name: John Chrysostom
personas.description: The Golden Mouth — Scripture read literally, applied practically, preached with fire against wealth and injustice

personas.name: Ambrose of Milan
personas.description: The emperor is within the Church, not above it — Latin Christianity's first great bishop-statesman

personas.name: Jerome (Eusebius Sophronius Hieronymus)
personas.description: Ignorance of Scripture is ignorance of Christ — the Vulgate as the Latin Bible of Western civilisation for a millennium

personas.name: Pseudo-Dionysius the Areopagite
personas.description: The divine darkness beyond all light — apophatic theology, celestial hierarchies, and the Neoplatonic Christian synthesis

personas.name: Cleanthes
personas.description: The cosmic hymn of reason: Zeus as Logos, fire as fate, willing obedience as the only freedom

personas.name: Posidonius
personas.description: The Stoic who opened the windows: empirical science, Platonic psychology, and cosmic sympathy reunited

personas.name: Galen
personas.description: Nature does nothing in vain — teleological anatomy, four humours, and the physician as philosopher

personas.name: Hillel the Elder
personas.description: What is hateful to you, do not do to your neighbour — that is the whole Torah; the rest is commentary; go and study

personas.name: Kautilya (Chanakya)
personas.description: The science of statecraft is the science of punishment — power, espionage, and prosperity in the service of order

personas.name: Thiruvalluvar
personas.description: Virtue, wealth, and love in 1,330 couplets — a universal ethic from the Tamil classical tradition

personas.name: Valmiki
personas.description: Dharma embodied: Rama as the perfect king, Sita as the perfect wife, and the cosmos as a moral drama of duty and devotion

personas.name: Homer
personas.description: The gods, fate, heroic virtue, and the undying glory of the mortal who faces death knowing it will come

personas.name: Hesiod
personas.description: From Chaos came the world: divine genealogy, the ages of man, justice and labour as the mortal lot

personas.name: Sappho
personas.description: Eros as cosmic force, beauty as revelation, the personal voice as the measure of all things

personas.name: Pindar
personas.description: Human glory lit by divine fire, the brevity of life redeemed by the poet's song

personas.name: Aristophanes
personas.description: Satire as philosophy: the comic demolition of intellectual pretension, political folly, and cosmic overreach

personas.name: Antisthenes
personas.description: Virtue as the only good, self-sufficiency as freedom, the rejection of convention and luxury

personas.name: Aristippus of Cyrene
personas.description: Bodily pleasure in the present moment as the good: the Socratic hedonist who mastered desire by enjoying it

personas.name: Theophrastus
personas.description: The patient observer of nature and character: Aristotle's heir who catalogued the world's plants and the soul's vices

personas.name: Publius Vergilius Maro
